meta.note: EXAMPLE CONTENT - two fictional projects, shipped so the Grill Room works out of the box and shows you the shape. Copy this file to grill.s3.json and replace both projects with your own. Every id must be prefixed with the project id and a dash (proj-c1, proj-q01, proj-w1, proj-l1) because the app derives project ownership from that prefix. Write your dossier the way these are written: concrete numbers, real weaknesses named before the interviewer finds them, and answers that concede rather than spin. A dossier that flatters you is worse than no dossier - the room will find what you hid from yourself.

projects.name: Linkbase - URL Shortener Service (EXAMPLE)
projects.coach_brief:
ROLE: you are an adversarial-but-fair technical interviewer grilling the candidate on 'Linkbase', their URL-shortener service. Ask ONE question at a time, follow the weakest thing they just said, and never accept a number without asking how it was measured. This is EXAMPLE dossier content shipped with the app; a real candidate should have replaced it with their own project.

WHAT IT IS: Linkbase is a single-binary URL shortener in Go 1.22 (chi router, pgx/v5, go-redis/v9), ~4,200 LOC, backed by Postgres 15 and Redis 7, deployed as one container on a 2-vCPU / 4 GB VPS behind a CDN. Two paths: a write path (POST /api/links mints a short code) and a read path (GET /{code} issues a redirect). The design assumption is that reads outnumber writes roughly 100:1, so essentially every decision optimizes the redirect. Schema: links(code text primary key, long_url text, owner_id uuid, created_at timestamptz, expires_at timestamptz null, revoked bool default false) and click_events(id bigserial, code text, ts timestamptz, ip_hash bytea, ua_family text, referer_host text). Demo dataset: ~180,000 links, ~9.1M click rows.

KEY GENERATION - press the story: the candidate first shipped hash-based codes (top 42 bits of SHA-256(long_url), base62-encoded to 7 chars) because identical URLs deduplicate for free. They replaced it with 7 random base62 characters from crypto/rand plus INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (code) DO NOTHING and a retry loop (3 attempts, then widen to 8 chars). Their stated reasons: (a) a hash makes the code a pure function of the URL, so anyone can test whether a given URL was ever shortened - an enumeration and privacy leak; (b) dedup becomes a misfeature once links can expire, be revoked, or carry per-owner analytics, because two owners then share one code; (c) custom aliases collide with the hash space anyway. Keyspace math they should be able to do cold: 62^7 = 3.52e12; at 10M links the expected number of collisions is n^2/(2N) ~ 14 in total and the per-insert conflict probability is ~3e-6. They should also be able to explain the third option they rejected - a Postgres sequence base62-encoded, which has zero collisions but produces sequential, trivially enumerable codes unless you push the counter through a format-preserving permutation (a small Feistel network or a multiplicative inverse mod 62^7).

READ PATH: cache-aside on Redis. GET /{code} checks Redis (key l:{code}, value = long_url, TTL 24h with +/-10% jitter); on a miss it queries Postgres by primary key and back-fills. Misses that resolve to nothing are negatively cached for 60s (key n:{code}) so a scanner hammering random codes cannot turn into a Postgres scan. Stampede control is golang.org/x/sync/singleflight keyed by code, so N concurrent misses on the same hot code collapse into one database query per process. There is also probabilistic early expiration (XFetch): a request refreshes the entry early when now - delta * beta * ln(rand()) >= expiry, where delta is the measured recompute time, so hot keys get renewed before they ever expire and cold keys almost never do.

REDIRECT SEMANTICS: 302 Found with Cache-Control: private, max-age=0, not 301. Their reasoning is correct and worth making them state: a 301 is cached by browsers and intermediaries effectively forever, which makes revocation and expiry unenforceable and makes click analytics silently undercount after the first visit per browser. The cost they accept is that every visit hits their origin.

RATE LIMITING: a token bucket implemented as a Redis Lua script (atomic read-refill-decrement in one round trip): 20 writes/min per API key, 600 redirects/min per client IP, burst = bucket capacity.

ANALYTICS WRITE PATH: the redirect handler pushes an event onto a buffered channel (capacity 8,192) and returns; a background writer batches into click_events every 200ms or 500 rows, whichever first. If the channel is full the event is DROPPED, not blocked.

THE NUMBER THEY QUOTE: 12,000 redirects/sec at p50 1.2ms / p99 9ms, measured with k6.

LANDMINES, in the order to press them:
1. THE BENCHMARK. The 12,000 rps run was executed on the candidate's laptop (8-core, 32 GB) with Postgres, Redis, the service, AND the k6 load generator all on the same host over loopback: no network, no TLS, no CDN, a 1,000-URL working set that is 100% resident in cache after warm-up, and a load generator competing for the same CPU. It is a code-path microbenchmark, not a capacity number. Do not let them quote it as throughput until they say that themselves.
2. '99.9% UPTIME'. The README says it. There is no uptime monitor, no measurement window, no SLO definition, and deploys are a docker restart with no connection draining. The number is invented. Make them retract it explicitly.
3. THE CACHE-INVALIDATION RACE. Revoke does DELETE-from-Postgres then DEL-from-Redis. A concurrent reader can miss in Redis, read the still-visible row from Postgres, and then SET it into Redis AFTER the DEL lands - resurrecting a revoked link for a full 24h TTL. The candidate identified this by reading the code and has NEVER reproduced it: no test, no observed incident, no log evidence. Ask what fixes exist (delayed double-delete, a short tombstone key the read path honours, version-stamped cache keys, or invalidation driven off Postgres logical decoding so one writer owns the order) and why they picked none of them.
4. click_events IS UNBOUNDED. No partitioning, no retention, no rollups. At ~1.2M clicks/day and ~120 bytes/row plus indexes that is roughly 250 MB/month; the per-code count query is already a full index scan and the dashboard degrades from ~40ms to seconds once the index stops fitting in shared_buffers.
5. TESTS ARE HAPPY PATH ONLY. 41 tests, ~62% coverage on handlers and 0% on the cache layer. Nothing tests the ON CONFLICT retry, expiry, revocation, the negative cache, or any concurrent interleaving.
6. RATE-LIMITER IDENTITY. The per-IP limiter reads X-Forwarded-For without a trusted-proxy allowlist, so the client can spoof it and get an unlimited number of buckets. Also: buckets live in one Redis with no cluster, so Redis is a single point of failure for the limiter AND the cache.
7. REDIS AS A HARD DEPENDENCY. Steady-state cache hit rate is 96.8%, so a cold or unavailable Redis sends ~30x the current query volume at a single unreplicated Postgres primary. There is no circuit breaker and no load-shedding, and this scenario was never tested.
8. ABUSE. A public shortener is a phishing laundromat. There is no domain blocklist, no reputation check, no abuse-report path, and no scan of the destination.
9. LOSSY ANALYTICS. Events drop silently when the buffer fills, which happens precisely during traffic spikes - so the counts are least accurate exactly when they matter, and nothing in the UI marks them as approximate.

CALIBRATION: a STRONG answer states the flaw before you finish the question, gives the specific mechanism (not 'there could be a race' but the exact interleaving), quantifies the impact, and names what they would change and what they would need to measure to know it worked. A WEAK answer re-quotes the 12,000 rps, says 'it's just a side project', or invents a defence on the spot. Reward correct arithmetic done live (62^7, birthday collisions, cache-hit maths, capacity de-rating). Punish any number they cannot say the measurement method for. They should be able to defend the genuinely good calls - random codes over hashed, 302 over 301, singleflight, negative caching, the Lua token bucket - crisply and without hedging; if they apologise for those too, they are not calibrated, they are just self-flagellating. Push until you find something they will actually defend.

pitches.s30: Linkbase is a URL shortener I wrote in Go, backed by Postgres with Redis in front of it. It mints seven-character random base62 codes with a conflict-retry on insert, and serves redirects cache-aside with singleflight so a hot link never stampedes the database. It's a read-heavy design - about a hundred reads per write - so almost every decision optimizes the redirect path: 302 instead of 301 so revocation and analytics actually work, negative caching so scanners can't turn misses into database load, and a Redis-Lua token bucket for rate limiting. I have a laptop benchmark at twelve thousand redirects a second, which is a code-path number, not a capacity number - it ran with the database on the same host.
pitches.m2: Linkbase is a URL shortener: about 4,200 lines of Go, Postgres 15 for durability, Redis 7 as a cache-aside layer, one binary on a small VPS. The interesting decisions are all on the read path, because reads outnumber writes roughly a hundred to one. Key generation: I started with hash-based codes - truncated SHA-256 of the URL - because it deduplicates for free. I replaced it with seven random base62 characters plus INSERT ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING and a retry, for two reasons. First, a hash makes the code a function of the URL, so anyone can test whether a URL was ever shortened, which is a real privacy leak. Second, dedup breaks the moment links can expire or be revoked per owner, because two owners end up sharing one code. Sixty-two to the seventh is three and a half trillion codes, so at ten million links you'd expect about fourteen collisions total - the retry loop is a correctness guarantee that essentially never fires. Reads: check Redis, on a miss query Postgres by primary key and back-fill with a jittered 24-hour TTL. Misses are negatively cached for a minute so a scanner can't convert random codes into database scans, and concurrent misses on the same code collapse through singleflight into a single query, with probabilistic early expiry so hot keys get refreshed before they ever go cold. Redirects are 302, not 301, because a 301 is cached forever by browsers and would make revocation unenforceable and undercount every click after the first. Analytics are fire-and-forget onto a buffered channel and batch-inserted, so the redirect path never waits on a write. The honest limits: my throughput number came from a laptop with the load generator and the database on the same host, so it measures code paths, not capacity; the click table has no retention policy; the tests are happy-path; and there's an invalidation race on revoke that I found by reading the code and have never actually reproduced.
pitches.m10:
- Problem and shape: shortening is trivial; the interesting part is a read-heavy, low-latency, abuse-magnet system - ~100:1 reads:writes, so optimize GET /{code} and nothing else
- Stack and size: Go 1.22 (chi, pgx/v5, go-redis/v9), ~4,200 LOC, Postgres 15, Redis 7, single container on a 2-vCPU VPS; ~180k links / ~9.1M click rows in the demo data
- Key generation, three options: truncated-hash (free dedup, but the code is a function of the URL - enumerable and a privacy leak), sequence + base62 (zero collisions, but sequential codes are scrapeable unless you Feistel-permute them), random base62 + ON CONFLICT retry (what I shipped)
- The collision maths, out loud: 62^7 = 3.52e12; expected collisions at n links = n^2/(2N); at 10M links that is ~14 total, per-insert conflict ~3e-6
- Schema and indexes: code as the primary key (the lookup is the key - no secondary index on the hot path); expires_at and revoked checked at read time, not by a sweeper
- Read path end to end: Redis GET -> miss -> singleflight -> Postgres PK lookup -> back-fill with 24h TTL +/- 10% jitter -> 302; negative cache for 60s on not-found; XFetch probabilistic early expiry so hot keys renew before expiring
- 302 vs 301: 301 is cached by browsers effectively forever - revocation stops working and clicks undercount after the first visit per browser; I pay origin traffic for control
- Rate limiting: token bucket as a Redis Lua script so refill-and-decrement is one atomic round trip; 20 writes/min per key, 600 redirects/min per IP - and the identity is X-Forwarded-For with no trusted-proxy allowlist, which is spoofable and is a bug
- Analytics: buffered channel (8,192) -> batch insert every 200ms or 500 rows; drops on full buffer, so counts are lossy exactly during spikes
- The number, framed honestly: 12,000 rps / p99 9ms from k6 on my laptop with Postgres, Redis and the load generator on the same host over loopback, 1,000-URL working set, 100% cache hit - a code-path benchmark, not capacity; a real 2-vCPU box with TLS and network is a different, unmeasured number
- What I would not defend: the '99.9% uptime' line in the README (never measured), the unbounded click_events table, happy-path-only tests, and an invalidation race on revoke I found by reading and never reproduced
- Next three things, in order: reproduce the race with a deterministic interleaving test, partition click_events monthly with 90-day retention plus a rollup, then re-run the load test off-box with TLS to get a number I can actually quote

read.body: Linkbase is a URL-shortener service: POST a long URL, get back a 7-character code; GET /{code} redirects. ~4,200 LOC of Go 1.22 (chi router, pgx/v5, go-redis/v9) shipped as a single container on a 2-vCPU / 4 GB VPS behind a CDN, with Postgres 15 for durability and Redis 7 as a cache. Two tables: links(code text primary key, long_url text, owner_id uuid, created_at, expires_at null, revoked bool) and click_events(id bigserial, code, ts, ip_hash bytea, ua_family, referer_host). Demo dataset: ~180,000 links and ~9.1M click rows. The whole design rests on one assumption - reads outnumber writes about 100:1 - so the redirect path gets every optimization and the write path gets almost none.

read.h: Key generation: three options, one shipped
read.body:
Option A, hash-based: take the top 42 bits of SHA-256(long_url), base62-encode to 7 characters. Identical URLs deduplicate for free and the mapping is stateless. Rejected because the code becomes a pure function of the URL - anyone can compute the code for a URL and test whether it was ever shortened (an oracle, and a privacy leak) - and because dedup actively breaks once links have owners, expiry, and revocation, since two owners would share one code and one revocation would kill both.

Option B, counter-based: a Postgres sequence, base62-encoded. Zero collisions by construction and the shortest possible codes. Rejected because the codes are sequential and the entire corpus can be walked by incrementing. The fix exists - push the counter through a length-preserving pseudorandom permutation (a small Feistel network, or multiply by an odd constant and take the inverse mod 62^7) so the output looks random while staying a bijection - but that is real cryptographic surface for a side project.

Option C, shipped: 7 random base62 characters from crypto/rand, then INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (code) DO NOTHING; if zero rows were inserted, retry (3 attempts, then widen to 8 characters). The database's unique index is the only arbiter of uniqueness, so no read-then-write race exists. Keyspace 62^7 = 3.52e12. Expected collisions at n links = n^2/(2N): at 10M links, ~14 collisions in the entire lifetime of the corpus, per-insert conflict probability ~3e-6. The retry loop is a correctness guarantee that essentially never executes - which is exactly why it has never been exercised by a test.

read.h: The read path and the cache
read.body:
Cache-aside on Redis. GET /{code}: read l:{code}; on hit, 302 immediately. On miss, take a singleflight lease keyed by the code so N concurrent misses on the same code become one Postgres query per process; query links by primary key; if found and not revoked and not expired, SET l:{code} with a 24h TTL plus or minus 10% jitter (so a batch of keys cached together does not all expire in the same second) and redirect. If not found, write n:{code} with a 60s TTL - the negative cache - so a scanner walking random codes cannot convert misses into unbounded Postgres traffic.

Stampede control has a second layer: probabilistic early expiration (XFetch). On each hit the handler computes now - delta * beta * ln(rand()) and refreshes early if that crosses the expiry time, where delta is the measured recompute cost and beta is a tunable (1.0 here). Hot keys therefore get renewed slightly before they expire, so a popular link never has a moment where the cache is empty; cold keys almost never trigger it. Steady-state hit rate on the demo traffic (Zipfian, alpha 0.9) is 96.8%.

The redirect is 302 Found with Cache-Control: private, max-age=0. A 301 Moved Permanently is cached by browsers and intermediaries essentially forever - clearing it requires clearing the user's browser cache. That would make two features unimplementable: revocation (a revoked link keeps working for everyone who has visited it) and expiry; and it would silently break analytics, because after the first visit a browser stops asking the origin at all, so every subsequent click is invisible. The cost of 302 is that every single visit hits the origin, which is precisely why the cache and the negative cache exist. This is the one decision in the project where the 'slower' choice is unambiguously correct.

Token bucket implemented as a Redis Lua script, so read-refill-decrement happens atomically in one round trip - the alternative, GET then SET from the application, is a lost-update race under concurrency. Limits: 20 writes/min per API key, 600 redirects/min per client IP; bucket capacity is the burst allowance and refill is continuous (tokens = min(cap, tokens + elapsed * rate)). Token bucket over fixed-window counters because a fixed window allows a 2x burst across the boundary; over sliding-window-log because the log costs memory proportional to request count.

Two known holes. The client identity for the per-IP bucket comes from X-Forwarded-For with no trusted-proxy allowlist, so a caller can spoof the header and mint an unlimited number of buckets - the limiter is currently decorative against a motivated attacker. And there is no abuse control at all on the content side: no destination blocklist, no reputation or safe-browsing check, no abuse-report endpoint. A public shortener is a phishing-laundering tool by default; this one has no defence.

The redirect handler must not wait on a write, so it pushes a struct onto a buffered channel (capacity 8,192) and returns. A single background goroutine drains it and batch-INSERTs into click_events every 200ms or every 500 rows, whichever comes first - batching because one multi-row INSERT costs roughly one round trip and one WAL flush instead of 500 of each. If the channel is full, the event is dropped and a counter increments.

Two honest consequences. First, the pipeline is lossy by design, and it loses events precisely during traffic spikes - the moments the numbers matter most - while the dashboard presents the counts as exact. Second, click_events has no partitioning and no retention: at ~1.2M clicks/day, ~120 bytes/row plus index, that is roughly 250 MB/month growing forever. The per-code count query is an index scan whose cost grows with history, so the dashboard degrades from ~40ms to seconds once the index no longer fits in shared_buffers. The fix is monthly range partitions, a rollup table of (code, day, count), and dropping raw partitions after 90 days - none of which is built.

read.h: The load test, and what it does not mean
read.body:
k6, 200 virtual users, 60s, 1,000-URL working set: 12,000 redirects/sec sustained, p50 1.2ms, p99 9ms, 0 errors, 96.8% cache hit. Every one of those conditions inflates the number: the service, Postgres, Redis and the load generator all ran on the same 8-core laptop over loopback, so there is no network latency, no TLS handshake cost, and no CDN in the path; the load generator competed with the service for CPU (which biases the number down) while loopback and a fully-cached 1,000-URL working set biased it up much harder; the working set fit entirely in Redis, so Postgres was almost never touched; and nothing was measured for longer than a minute, so no cache eviction, no connection-pool exhaustion, no WAL bloat, no GC pressure at steady state.

What it legitimately shows: the request-handling code path is not the bottleneck, and there is no accidental O(n) work per redirect. What it does NOT show: capacity, tail behaviour under contention, or anything about the 2-vCPU production box. An honest capacity estimate requires re-running off-box, with TLS, against a realistically sized working set, for long enough to see steady state - and would land far lower.

claims.claim: Random base62 with an ON CONFLICT retry is the right key scheme here, not hashing and not a counter.
claims.evidence: mint.go: 7 chars from crypto/rand, INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (code) DO NOTHING, 3 retries then widen to 8. Keyspace 62^7 = 3.52e12; expected collisions at 10M links ~14 (n^2/2N); per-insert conflict ~3e-6.
claims.limitation: The retry path has never executed in production or in a test - it is unverified code on the correctness-critical path, and I only know it compiles.
claims.counter: You shipped a branch that never runs and called it a guarantee. A counter with a Feistel permutation would give zero collisions AND unguessable codes, with no retry path to be wrong about.
claims.wording: I chose random codes over hashing because a hash makes the code a function of the URL, which lets anyone test whether a URL was ever shortened, and because dedup breaks once links have owners and revocation. I chose them over a sequence because sequential codes are enumerable. The unique index is the sole arbiter, so there is no read-then-write race - and I will concede the retry branch has never actually fired, so it is untested code.

claims.claim: 302 rather than 301 is correct even though it costs an origin hit on every visit.
claims.evidence: redirect.go returns 302 with Cache-Control: private, max-age=0; revocation and expiry are enforced at read time and depend on the browser asking again.
claims.limitation: It means the origin can never be bypassed for the hot path, so my availability floor is the service's availability - a 301 would have let the browser serve the redirect while I was down.
claims.counter: For permanent marketing links a 301 is both faster and more cacheable, and you have thrown away edge caching for features most links never use.
claims.wording: A 301 is cached by browsers effectively forever, so revocation stops working and every click after the first becomes invisible to analytics. I traded edge cacheability for the ability to actually revoke a link and count it - for a shortener, where abuse takedown matters, that is the right side of the trade.

claims.claim: Singleflight plus jittered TTLs plus probabilistic early expiry removes cache stampede on hot keys.
claims.evidence: cache.go: x/sync/singleflight keyed by code; TTL 24h +/- 10%; XFetch refresh when now - delta*beta*ln(rand) >= expiry.
claims.limitation: Singleflight is per-process. With N application instances a hot-key expiry produces N simultaneous Postgres queries, not one - it reduces the stampede by the in-process concurrency factor, it does not eliminate it.
claims.counter: That is not stampede protection, it is stampede attenuation. A distributed lock or a single-writer refresh would actually bound the database to one query.
claims.wording: Within one process singleflight collapses concurrent misses to a single query, jitter stops synchronized expiry, and XFetch means hot keys get refreshed before they ever go cold - so in practice the hot key never expires under load. The honest bound is that it is per-process: at N instances you get N queries, not one, and a distributed lease would be the real fix.

claims.claim: The service sustains 12,000 redirects/sec at p99 9ms.
claims.evidence: k6, 200 VUs, 60s, 1,000-URL working set, 0 errors, 96.8% cache hit.
claims.limitation: It was measured on my laptop with the service, Postgres, Redis and the load generator on the same host over loopback: no network, no TLS, no CDN, a fully cached working set, and a 60-second window that never reaches steady state.
claims.counter: Then the number describes your laptop's loopback, not your service. On the 2-vCPU production box with TLS, it could plausibly be an order of magnitude lower and you would have no idea.
claims.wording: I should state that as a code-path benchmark, not a capacity number. It ran on one laptop with the database, cache and load generator on the same host over loopback, with a working set that fit entirely in cache. What it honestly shows is that there is no accidental per-request work dominating the handler. What it can't tell you is production capacity - to claim that I would have to re-run it off-box, with TLS, against a realistic working set, long enough to reach steady state.

claims.evidence: None. The line is in the README and nothing produced it.
claims.limitation: There is no external monitor, no measurement window, no SLO definition, and deploys are a container restart with no connection draining - which alone would blow that budget.
claims.counter: A number in a README with no measurement behind it is not an approximation, it is a fabrication - what else in this README was written from vibes?
claims.wording: That line is not measured and I should not have written it - there is no uptime monitor and no defined window, so I have no basis for the number. I am removing it. If I wanted to claim availability I would need an external prober, a stated SLO with a measurement window, and deploys that drain connections instead of restarting the container.

claims.claim: Rate limiting is correct under concurrency because the token bucket is a single atomic Lua script.
claims.evidence: ratelimit.lua: reads tokens and last-refill, computes tokens = min(cap, tokens + elapsed*rate), decrements and writes back in one Redis round trip.
claims.limitation: The algorithm is atomic but the identity is not trustworthy: the per-IP bucket key is derived from X-Forwarded-For with no trusted-proxy allowlist, so a client can spoof the header and get a fresh bucket per request.
claims.counter: An atomic limiter keyed on an attacker-controlled string limits nobody. You solved the easy half.
claims.wording: The counting is right - doing it in Lua makes refill-and-decrement atomic instead of a lost-update race between GET and SET. The identity is wrong: I take X-Forwarded-For without a trusted-proxy allowlist, so it is spoofable and the per-IP limit is effectively decorative. The fix is to parse XFF right-to-left and only trust hops inside my known proxy set, falling back to the socket address.

claims.claim: Analytics never slow the redirect path.
claims.evidence: The handler does a non-blocking send onto a buffered channel (8,192) and returns; a background writer batch-inserts every 200ms or 500 rows.
claims.limitation: It is achieved by dropping events when the buffer fills, and the buffer fills during exactly the spikes worth measuring. Nothing surfaces the drop counter and the dashboard presents counts as exact.
claims.counter: So your click counts are silently wrong, and most wrong when traffic is most interesting. That is worse than slow analytics - it is confidently incorrect analytics.
claims.wording: Decoupling the write was the right call - a redirect should never wait on an analytics insert. But the failure mode is silent loss under load, and I present the counts as exact, which is wrong. Minimum fix: expose the dropped-event counter, label the dashboard as approximate, and either spill to disk or shed to a sampled counter instead of dropping blind.

claims.evidence: The application is stateless; all shared state lives in Redis and Postgres, so instances can be added behind the load balancer freely.
claims.limitation: The stateless tier is the easy part. Postgres is a single primary with no read replica, Redis is a single node with no cluster or replica, and both the cache and the rate limiter depend on that one Redis - so the system scales to exactly one database and one cache.
claims.counter: 'Stateless app' is not a scaling story, it is a prerequisite. Where does the next 10x come from when the primary is saturated?
claims.wording: The app tier scales; the data tier does not. One Postgres primary, one Redis, no replicas, and the cache and limiter share that Redis - so the honest ceiling is whatever a single primary serves. The next real step is a read replica for the redirect lookup, since it is a primary-key read of immutable-ish rows and can tolerate replica lag, plus Redis replication so a cache loss does not become a database incident.

claims.claim: The test suite is good enough to refactor against.
claims.limitation: Every test is happy path. Coverage on the cache layer is 0%. Nothing exercises the ON CONFLICT retry, expiry, revocation, the negative cache, or any concurrent interleaving - so the suite cannot catch the one bug I already know about.
claims.counter: 62% coverage of the paths that were never going to break is not confidence, it is decoration.
claims.wording: No - the suite covers the paths that work and none of the paths I am worried about. There is no test for the conflict retry, none for revocation or expiry, and zero coverage of the cache layer, which is where the race I know about lives. The first test I would write is a deterministic interleaving that reproduces the revoke race, because right now I cannot even prove it exists.

qa.q: Encode and decode base62 by hand. Why base62 and not base64?
qa.a: Base62 uses [0-9A-Za-z]. Encoding an integer n: repeatedly take n mod 62 as the next digit and n = n div 62, then reverse. Decoding: fold left, acc = acc*62 + digitValue(c). Base64's extra characters are '+' and '/' (or '-' and '_'), and '+' and '/' require percent-encoding in a URL path, which defeats the point of a short link; base62 is the largest alphabet that is URL-safe with no escaping and no case-folding surprises. The cost is that 62 is not a power of two, so encoding needs division rather than bit-shifting - irrelevant at this scale.

qa.q: Why 7 characters? Do the keyspace and collision maths out loud.
qa.a: 62^7 = 3.52e12. With n links in a keyspace of N, the expected number of collisions is approximately n^2/(2N): at 10M links that is 1e14/7.04e12 ~ 14 collisions in total, and the probability that any single insert conflicts is n/N ~ 3e-6. So 7 characters supports tens of millions of links with a retry that essentially never fires. Six characters (5.68e10) would give n^2/2N ~ 880 collisions at 10M - still handleable, but the code widens to 8 on repeated conflict anyway, so 7 is the point where the URL stays short and the retry stays theoretical.

qa.q: You started with hash-based codes and moved to random. What broke?
qa.a: Two things. Privacy: the code is a pure function of the URL, so anyone can hash a URL and probe whether it was shortened - that is an oracle over other people's links. And ownership: once links have owners, expiry and revocation, dedup means two owners share one row, so one person's revocation kills the other person's link and their analytics are merged. Dedup is only a feature when links are anonymous and immutable, which stopped being true the moment I added accounts.

qa.q: Why not a Postgres sequence base62-encoded? It has zero collisions.
qa.a: It does, and it produces the shortest possible codes - but sequential codes are enumerable, so anyone can walk the entire corpus by incrementing. You can fix that by pushing the counter through a length-preserving pseudorandom permutation - a small Feistel network over the 62^7 domain, or multiplying by an odd constant with a known modular inverse - which keeps the bijection (still zero collisions) while making the output look random. I judged that to be more cryptographic surface than a side project should carry when a retry loop that fires once in 300,000 inserts solves it.

qa.q: How do you guarantee uniqueness without a read-then-write race?
qa.a: I never read to check. The insert is INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (code) DO NOTHING and I check the rows-affected count: 0 means someone else owns that code, so I generate a new one and retry. The unique index on the primary key is the single arbiter, so uniqueness is decided inside one atomic statement. A SELECT-then-INSERT would be a classic TOCTOU: two writers can both see 'free' and both try to insert.

qa.q: Walk the redirect path from request to response.
qa.a: Parse the code, GET l:{code} from Redis. Hit: emit 302, push the click event onto the channel, return. Miss: check the negative-cache key n:{code}; if present, 404 immediately. Otherwise enter singleflight keyed by the code so concurrent misses share one lookup, SELECT long_url, revoked, expires_at FROM links WHERE code = $1 - a primary-key lookup. If missing or revoked or expired, SET n:{code} with 60s TTL and 404. Otherwise SET l:{code} with a 24h TTL jittered +/-10%, then 302. Steady-state hit rate on Zipfian traffic is 96.8%, so about 1 in 30 requests reaches Postgres.

qa.q: What is a cache stampede and which mechanism actually prevents it here?
qa.a: A stampede is many concurrent requests missing the same key at the same instant and all recomputing it - a hot key expiring turns into a burst of identical database queries, which can be enough to knock the database over exactly when the item is most popular. Three mechanisms, doing different jobs: TTL jitter prevents synchronized expiry across keys cached together; singleflight collapses simultaneous misses on one key into one lookup per process; and probabilistic early expiry (XFetch) means a hot key gets refreshed slightly before it expires, so under real load the hot key never has an empty moment at all. Only XFetch actually prevents the miss; singleflight limits the damage when one happens.

qa.q: Explain the XFetch formula and why it is scaled by recompute time.
qa.a: On each hit, refresh early if now - delta * beta * ln(rand()) >= expiry, where delta is the measured cost of recomputing the value, beta is a tunable (1.0), and rand() is uniform on (0,1]. Since ln of a value in (0,1] is negative, the term is a positive random offset that grows as you approach expiry, so the probability of an early refresh rises smoothly toward the deadline. Scaling by delta is the key idea: expensive-to-recompute values get refreshed further ahead of expiry, because they are the ones where a miss hurts. It is probabilistic, so different clients pick different moments instead of all refreshing at once.

qa.q: Why negatively cache misses, and what is the risk of doing so?
qa.a: Without it, a scanner requesting random 7-character codes turns every request into a Postgres query - the cache is useless against traffic that by definition never hits. Negative caching with a 60s TTL makes that traffic cost one round trip to Redis. The risk is staleness in the other direction: if a code is created within that window, the first minute of requests to a link that now exists returns 404. I bound it by keeping the negative TTL short (60s vs 24h) and by deleting n:{code} on create.

qa.q: 301 versus 302. Argue both sides, then commit.
qa.a: 301 Moved Permanently is cached by browsers and intermediaries essentially indefinitely, so the second visit never touches my origin - lower latency and lower cost, ideal for permanent links. But it makes revocation and expiry unenforceable (you cannot clear a user's browser cache) and it silently destroys analytics, because every visit after the first is invisible. 302 Found with Cache-Control: private, max-age=0 forces every visit through me: I pay the origin hit, and in exchange revocation works, expiry works, and click counts are real. For a shortener - a service that will be used for phishing and where takedown must actually take down - control wins.

qa.q: Why is your rate limiter a Lua script instead of application code?
qa.a: Because refill-and-decrement must be atomic. In application code the sequence GET tokens, compute, SET tokens is a lost-update race: two concurrent requests both read 5 tokens, both write 4, and two requests cost one token. Redis executes a Lua script atomically against a single-threaded core, so the whole read-modify-write happens with no interleaving, in one network round trip. The alternatives - WATCH/MULTI with retries, or a distributed lock - cost more round trips and more failure modes for the same guarantee.

qa.q: Token bucket versus fixed window versus sliding window log. Why token bucket?
qa.a: A fixed-window counter allows a 2x burst across the boundary: 60 requests at 11:59:59 and 60 more at 12:00:00 pass a '60/min' limit. A sliding-window log is exact but stores a timestamp per request, so memory grows with traffic - the thing you are trying to control. A token bucket stores two numbers (tokens, last-refill) per key, refills continuously at rate r, and permits bursts up to the capacity, which matches real client behaviour - people arrive in bursts and you want to allow a reasonable one while bounding the sustained rate.

qa.q: Your per-IP limit reads X-Forwarded-For. What is wrong with that?
qa.a: X-Forwarded-For is a client-supplied header. Without a trusted-proxy allowlist, an attacker sets it to a random value per request and gets a fresh bucket every time, so the per-IP limit stops existing. The correct handling is to walk the header right-to-left, discarding entries as long as the hop is inside my known proxy/CDN CIDR set, and take the first untrusted address; if the connection did not come from a trusted proxy, ignore the header entirely and use the socket address. Mine does none of that, which makes that limit decorative.

qa.q: Why is the analytics write asynchronous and batched, and what does it cost?
qa.a: Synchronously inserting a click row would put a WAL flush on the redirect path and couple redirect availability to write availability - the redirect would fail because logging failed. So the handler does a non-blocking send onto a buffered channel and a background writer batches every 200ms or 500 rows: one multi-row INSERT costs roughly one round trip and one flush instead of 500 of each. The cost is loss: when the buffer is full, events are dropped, and the buffer fills during spikes. So the counts are approximate and least accurate under load, while the dashboard shows them as exact - that is a real defect, not a trade-off I get to be smug about.

qa.q: click_events grows forever. Walk me through what actually breaks and when.
qa.a: At ~1.2M clicks/day and ~120 bytes/row plus a btree on (code, ts), that is roughly 250 MB/month of heap plus index. Nothing breaks abruptly; it degrades. Per-code aggregate queries are index range scans whose cost scales with retained history, so the dashboard drifts from ~40ms toward seconds; once the index stops fitting in shared_buffers, those scans start hitting disk and the p99 falls off a cliff; autovacuum takes longer on the big table and can fall behind, and a table-rewrite migration becomes hours of downtime. The fix is monthly range partitioning plus a rollup table of (code, day, count) written by the batch writer, and dropping raw partitions after 90 days - dropping a partition is O(1) where DELETE would be a bloat generator.

qa.q: Redis dies right now. What happens?
qa.a: Every read misses, so 100% of redirect traffic goes to Postgres instead of the ~3.2% it sees today - roughly a 30x increase in query volume on a single unreplicated primary, plus every miss losing singleflight (which is per-process but still needs Redis for nothing - that part survives) and the negative cache disappearing, so scanner traffic hits the database too. The rate limiter also fails, and mine fails open. There is no circuit breaker and no load shedding, and I have never tested this. It is the single most likely way the service falls over, and the cheapest mitigations are a small in-process LRU as a second-level cache, an explicit fail-closed-or-shed policy for the limiter, and a concurrency cap on database lookups so a cache loss becomes higher latency rather than a database outage.

qa.q: Describe the cache-invalidation race in your revoke path precisely.
qa.a: Revoke does two steps: mark the row revoked in Postgres, then DEL l:{code} in Redis. The bad interleaving: reader misses the cache; reader SELECTs and gets the row in its pre-revoke state; revoker commits the update; revoker DELs the cache key; reader now SETs the stale value it read earlier, with a 24h TTL. The revoked link is resurrected for a day and no further revocation event will ever fire to clean it. The window is small - it is the gap between the reader's SELECT and its SET - but it is not zero, and it is exactly the window that opens under load.

qa.q: How would you fix that race, and what does each fix cost?
qa.a: Four options. (1) Delayed double delete: DEL, wait past the maximum plausible read window, DEL again - trivial to implement, probabilistic, and 'maximum plausible' is a guess. (2) Tombstone: on revoke write t:{code} with a TTL longer than any read window, and have the read path refuse to populate the cache when the tombstone exists - deterministic, costs one extra Redis read on the miss path only. (3) Version-stamped keys: keep a version counter per code, cache under l:{code}:{v}, bump on write - old entries become unreachable and expire on their own, at the cost of an extra lookup or a versioned prefix. (4) Single-writer invalidation via Postgres logical decoding: only the CDC consumer ever writes the cache, so ordering is decided by the WAL - correct by construction and by far the most infrastructure. For this project I would ship (2) and write the interleaving test first.

qa.q: You say you found that race by reading the code. Why does that matter?
qa.a: Because 'I reasoned about it' and 'I demonstrated it' are different confidence levels, and I have only the first. I have never reproduced it: no test, no log evidence, no incident. So I do not actually know the window's size, whether the revoke path even reaches the DEL under failure, or whether a fix works. The right move is a deterministic test that blocks the reader between SELECT and SET with a channel, runs the revoke, releases the reader, and asserts the key is absent - then the bug is a fact and the fix is verifiable. Until then it is a hypothesis I happen to believe.

qa.q: Give me an honest capacity estimate for the 2-vCPU production box.
qa.a: I can only give you a bounded guess, and I want to be explicit that it is one. The laptop number was 12,000 rps on 8 cores with no TLS and no network. De-rate for cores (2 vs 8, roughly 4x), for TLS termination and real socket handling on the hot path (call it 1.5-2x), and for a working set that will not be 100% cache-resident so a real fraction of requests pay a database round trip. That lands somewhere in the low thousands per second, and I would not quote a single figure from arithmetic. The measurement I would actually run: load generator on a separate host, TLS on, working set drawn from the real code distribution, 30 minutes to reach steady state, and report p99 at the load where errors begin rather than a peak throughput number.

qa.q: What stops your service from being a phishing tool?
qa.a: Nothing, today. There is no destination blocklist, no reputation or safe-browsing lookup at mint time, no abuse-report endpoint, no rescanning of previously-clean destinations, and no interstitial warning page. That is the largest real-world gap in the project - shorteners are attractive precisely because they hide the destination, and an open one with no takedown path will be abused within days of being public. The minimum viable answer is a reputation check at mint time, a revoke path that an abuse report can trigger (which is why 302 matters), an interstitial for destinations flagged after the fact, and a rescan of stored destinations, since a URL that was clean at mint time can be weaponised later.

qa.q: If you had one week on this, what three things would you do and in what order?
qa.a: One: write the deterministic interleaving test that reproduces the revoke race, then ship the tombstone fix - because it is a known correctness bug and everything else is performance or hygiene. Two: partition click_events monthly, add the (code, day, count) rollup, and set 90-day retention - because it is the failure that arrives on its own schedule whether or not I touch the code. Three: re-run the load test off-box with TLS and a realistic working set so I have a number I can say out loud, and delete the 99.9% uptime line from the README. Notably, none of those three is a feature - the project's problem is not that it does too little.

whiteboard.title: Base62 encode/decode, plus the collision probability from scratch
whiteboard.answer:
ENCODE(n): alphabet = '0123456789ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZabcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz' (62 symbols). While n > 0: emit alphabet[n mod 62], n = n div 62. Reverse the emitted digits. Zero is the special case - emit '0'. DECODE(s): acc = 0; for each char c, acc = acc*62 + index(c). It is plain positional notation in base 62; the only reason it is not base64 is that '+' and '/' need percent-encoding inside a URL path.

KEYSPACE: N = 62^7. Compute it stepwise: 62^2 = 3,844; 62^3 = 238,328; 62^4 ~ 1.478e7; 62^5 ~ 9.16e8; 62^6 ~ 5.68e10; 62^7 ~ 3.52e12.

COLLISIONS: with n codes drawn uniformly from N, the number of unordered pairs is C(n,2) = n(n-1)/2, and each pair collides with probability 1/N, so the expected number of colliding pairs is n(n-1)/(2N) ~ n^2/(2N) by linearity of expectation - no independence assumption needed. At n = 1e7: 1e14 / (2 * 3.52e12) ~ 14. The probability that a NEW insert conflicts with anything already stored is just n/N ~ 1e7/3.52e12 ~ 2.8e-6, i.e. about one conflict per 350,000 inserts. That is why 3 retries is overwhelming: the chance of three consecutive conflicts is ~2e-17.

BIRTHDAY BOUND (the related question): the n at which a collision becomes more likely than not is ~sqrt(2 ln2 * N) ~ 1.18*sqrt(N) ~ 2.2e6. Note the gap - past ~2.2M links you EXPECT some collision, which is exactly why the design handles it with a retry rather than pretending it cannot happen.

whiteboard.title: Write the read path in pseudocode, with every failure branch
whiteboard.answer:
get(code):
  v = redis.GET('l:'+code)
  if v != nil: emit_click(code); return 302 -> v
  if redis.EXISTS('n:'+code): return 404          # negative cache
  v, err = singleflight.Do(code, func():           # collapse concurrent misses
      row = db.QueryRow('SELECT long_url, revoked, expires_at FROM links WHERE code=$1', code)
      if no row or row.revoked or row.expires_at < now:
          redis.SET('n:'+code, 1, TTL=60s)
          return nil
      if redis.EXISTS('t:'+code): return row.long_url   # tombstone: serve, do NOT cache
      redis.SET('l:'+code, row.long_url, TTL=24h * (1 +/- 0.10))
      return row.long_url)
  if err != nil: return 503                        # do NOT cache an error
  if v == nil: return 404
  emit_click(code); return 302 -> v

emit_click(code): non-blocking send onto a buffered channel; on full, increment dropped_total and return - never block the redirect.

The branches that matter and are easy to get wrong: never populate the positive cache from an error path (that caches an outage); never let the negative cache outlive plausible create-then-fetch latency; the tombstone check must sit between the database read and the cache write, because that is precisely the window the revoke race lives in; and TTL jitter must be applied per key, not per batch, or you have just moved the synchronized expiry.

whiteboard.title: Derive the token-bucket algorithm and write it as an atomic script
whiteboard.answer:
STATE per key: tokens (float), last (timestamp). PARAMETERS: capacity C (max burst), refill rate r tokens/sec.

ON REQUEST at time now, cost 1:
  elapsed = max(0, now - last)
  tokens  = min(C, tokens + elapsed * r)     # continuous refill - no tick loop needed
  if tokens >= 1: tokens -= 1; last = now; ALLOW; retry_after = 0
  else:           last = now; DENY;  retry_after = (1 - tokens) / r
  persist(tokens, last) with an expiry of C/r seconds (an idle bucket is indistinguishable from a full one, so it can be evicted safely)

WHY IT MUST BE ATOMIC: as three separate commands (GET, compute, SET) two concurrent requests both read tokens=5, both write 4, and two requests have cost one token. Under load the limiter leaks in proportion to concurrency - the harder it is hit, the less it limits. In Redis, a Lua script runs to completion against a single-threaded core, so the read-modify-write cannot interleave, and it costs one round trip instead of three.

WHY NOT THE ALTERNATIVES: fixed window allows 2C across a boundary (C at the end of one window, C at the start of the next); sliding-window log is exact but stores one timestamp per request, so memory scales with the traffic you are trying to limit; leaky bucket smooths output but forbids bursts entirely, which is wrong for human traffic. Token bucket bounds the sustained rate at r while permitting a burst of C, in O(1) space.

DERIVED SANITY CHECK: with C = 600 and r = 10/sec (600/min), a client that has been idle can fire 600 requests instantly and then is pinned to 10/sec - which is the intended shape.

whiteboard.title: Draw the revoke race as an interleaving, then three fixes with their costs
whiteboard.answer:
THE INTERLEAVING (time flows down; R = reader, W = revoker):
  R: GET l:X            -> miss
  R: SELECT X           -> returns long_url, revoked=false        <-- reader now holds a value that is about to be false
  W: UPDATE links SET revoked=true WHERE code=X; COMMIT
  W: DEL l:X            -> deletes nothing (the key is not there yet)
  R: SET l:X = long_url TTL 24h                                    <-- the revoked link is now cached for a day
Net effect: a revoked URL keeps redirecting for up to 24 hours, and no further invalidation event will ever fire because revoke already ran. The window is the gap between the reader's SELECT and its SET - microseconds normally, far longer under GC pause, connection-pool wait, or network hiccup, i.e. exactly under load.

FIX 1 - DELAYED DOUBLE DELETE: DEL, sleep past the maximum plausible read window, DEL again. Cost: trivial; probabilistic; 'maximum plausible' is a guess and the second DEL can be lost if the process dies.
FIX 2 - TOMBSTONE: on revoke, SET t:X with a TTL comfortably longer than any read window; the read path checks t:X after the database read and refuses to populate the cache when it exists. Cost: one extra Redis read on the miss path only (~3% of requests). Deterministic as long as the tombstone TTL exceeds the read window. This is the one I would ship.
FIX 3 - VERSIONED KEYS: keep ver:X; cache under l:X:{ver}; bump ver on any write. Stale entries become unreachable and age out on their own. Cost: an extra lookup or a versioned prefix on every read; no window at all, because the old key can never be read again.
FIX 4 (the real-systems answer) - SINGLE-WRITER INVALIDATION: only a CDC consumer of the Postgres WAL writes or deletes cache entries, so the cache's order is the commit order by construction. Cost: a replication slot, a consumer to operate, and lag between commit and invalidation.

THE TEST THAT MAKES THIS REAL: inject a blocking channel between the reader's SELECT and its SET, run the revoke while the reader is parked, release the reader, then assert l:X is absent. Without that test, everything above is a story.

whiteboard.title: From a laptop benchmark to an honest capacity number
whiteboard.answer:
WHAT WAS MEASURED: 12,000 rps, p50 1.2ms, p99 9ms; k6, 200 VUs, 60s, 1,000-URL working set; service + Postgres + Redis + load generator all on one 8-core laptop over loopback.

EVERY BIAS, WITH ITS DIRECTION:
  loopback, no network RTT ................ inflates (removes ~0.2-1ms and all packet overhead per request)
  no TLS .................................. inflates (handshakes and per-record crypto are real CPU on the hot path)
  1,000-URL working set, 100% cached ...... inflates hard (Postgres is nearly never touched; real traffic has a long tail)
  no CDN / no proxy hop ................... inflates
  60s window .............................. inflates (no eviction, no pool exhaustion, no steady-state GC or WAL behaviour)
  load generator shares the CPU ........... deflates (the only bias in the honest direction, and much smaller than the rest)
  8 cores vs 2 in production .............. inflates ~4x on a CPU-bound path

DE-RATING, stated as arithmetic and not as truth: 12,000 / 4 (cores) / ~1.75 (TLS + real sockets) ~ 1,700 rps, before accounting for a working set that is not fully cache-resident. So: low thousands per second, with wide error bars, and I would not put a single number on a slide from this.

THE MEASUREMENT I WOULD ACTUALLY RUN: load generator on a separate host; TLS enabled; the working set sampled from the production code-popularity distribution (Zipf, so a genuine tail of cache misses); ramp until the error rate rises rather than searching for a peak throughput figure; run 30 minutes to reach steady state; report the load at which p99 crosses the SLO, not the maximum requests survived. Throughput without a latency objective is not a capacity number - it is the point at which the queue grew fastest.

whiteboard.title: Design click_events so it can run for years
whiteboard.answer:
TODAY: click_events(id bigserial, code text, ts timestamptz, ip_hash bytea, ua_family text, referer_host text) with a btree on (code, ts). One flat heap, no retention. ~1.2M rows/day, ~120 bytes/row plus index ~ 250 MB/month, forever.

TARGET DESIGN:
1. PARTITION BY RANGE (ts), one partition per month. Aggregates carry a ts predicate so the planner prunes to one or two partitions; retention becomes DROP TABLE on a partition - an O(1) catalogue operation - instead of a bulk DELETE, which would leave dead tuples for autovacuum and bloat the index without ever returning space.
2. ROLLUP TABLE click_daily(code text, day date, clicks int, primary key (code, day)). The batch writer already groups events in memory, so it can also emit INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (code, day) DO UPDATE SET clicks = click_daily.clicks + EXCLUDED.clicks in the same transaction. The dashboard reads the rollup only - a primary-key range scan over at most 365 rows per code, which is constant-cost regardless of how much raw history exists.
3. RETENTION: keep raw partitions 90 days (enough for debugging and for recomputing a rollup if the aggregation is wrong), keep click_daily indefinitely - it is roughly three orders of magnitude smaller.
4. PRIVACY, since it is the same decision: ip_hash should be a keyed hash with a rotating salt, not a bare hash, or the space of IPv4 addresses is small enough to brute-force back to the original. Rotating the salt monthly also makes the raw rows self-expiring in a useful sense.

WHY THIS ORDER: partitioning first, because it is the only change that makes retention cheap; the rollup second, because it is what actually fixes the dashboard's query cost; retention last, because it is a one-line cron once the first two exist.

landmines.q: You claim 12,000 redirects per second. Where exactly did you run that, and what was running next to it?
landmines.why: The bench ran on a laptop with the service, Postgres, Redis and the k6 load generator all on the same host over loopback, with a 1,000-URL working set that was 100% cache-resident. Quoting it as capacity is the fastest way to lose an interviewer's trust, because the follow-up questions are trivial to ask and impossible to survive.
landmines.answer: Facts: one 8-core laptop, everything on the same host over loopback - no network, no TLS, no CDN - 200 VUs for 60 seconds against a 1,000-URL working set that fit entirely in Redis, so Postgres was barely touched. Limitation: that measures my request-handling code path, not capacity. It legitimately shows there is no accidental per-request work dominating the handler; it says nothing about the 2-vCPU production box, tail behaviour under contention, or steady state. Differently: I would run the generator on a separate host with TLS on, draw the working set from the real code-popularity distribution so misses actually happen, ramp until errors appear rather than hunting a peak, and report the load at which p99 crosses an SLO. Until I do that, the honest sentence is 'the handler is not the bottleneck', not a throughput figure.

landmines.q: Your README says 99.9% uptime. Over what window, measured by what?
landmines.why: There is no monitor, no window, and no SLO - the number was written because it sounded like the right number. An unmeasured metric in a README invites the interviewer to assume every other number is also decorative.
landmines.answer: Facts: nothing measured it. There is no external prober, no defined measurement window, and no SLO - deploys are a container restart with no connection draining, which by itself would consume that error budget. Limitation: I wrote a number I could not support, which is worse than writing none, because it makes my real numbers less believable. Differently: I have removed the line. To claim availability I would need an external prober hitting a health endpoint from outside my network, a stated window and objective, and deploys that drain connections - and then I would quote the measured figure, whatever it turned out to be.

landmines.q: Walk me through your revoke path. Are you sure a revoked link stays revoked?
landmines.why: There is a real invalidation race - a reader can populate the cache with a value it read before the revoke committed, after the DEL has already run, resurrecting the link for a full 24h TTL. The candidate found it by reading the code and has never reproduced it, so the temptation is either to hide it or to overstate confidence in the diagnosis.
landmines.answer: Facts: no, not guaranteed. Revoke updates Postgres then DELs the cache key. If a reader missed the cache and read the row just before the commit, its SET can land after the DEL and cache the stale value for 24 hours - and no further invalidation will ever fire, because revoke already ran. Limitation: I found this by reading the code, not by observing it. I have no test, no log evidence, and no incident, so I cannot tell you how wide the window is in practice or whether the revoke path even reaches the DEL under failure. Differently: first a deterministic test that parks the reader between its SELECT and its SET, runs the revoke, then asserts the key is absent - so the bug becomes a fact instead of a hypothesis - then a tombstone key the read path honours before it populates the cache, which is cheap and deterministic where a delayed double-delete is only probabilistic.

landmines.q: How large is click_events after two years, and what breaks first?
landmines.why: No partitioning, no retention, no rollups. The candidate may not have a number - and 'it grows' without an estimate signals they never thought about the operational lifetime of their own schema.
landmines.answer: Facts: at ~1.2M clicks/day and ~120 bytes per row plus index, roughly 250 MB/month, so on the order of 6 GB of raw events plus index after two years, growing forever with no retention policy. What breaks first is the dashboard: the per-code aggregate is an index range scan whose cost grows with retained history, so it drifts from ~40ms toward seconds, and once the index no longer fits in shared_buffers those scans hit disk and p99 collapses. Autovacuum on the big table falls behind, and any table-rewrite migration becomes hours of downtime. Limitation: I designed the write path for throughput and never designed the read path for a table that only grows. Differently: monthly range partitions so retention is a DROP rather than a bloat-generating DELETE, a (code, day, count) rollup written by the same batch writer so the dashboard reads a constant-size range, and 90-day retention on raw partitions.

landmines.q: You have 41 tests. Which one would have caught the bug you just described to me?
landmines.why: None of them. Every test is happy path, cache-layer coverage is 0%, and the conflict retry, expiry, revocation and negative cache are all untested. This question exposes whether the candidate understands that coverage percentage is not the same as risk coverage.
landmines.answer: Facts: none of them would. All 41 are happy path, the cache layer has zero coverage, and there is no test for the ON CONFLICT retry, expiry, revocation, or any concurrent interleaving. The 62% statement coverage is on handlers, and it covers the paths that were never going to break. Limitation: a suite that cannot catch the one bug I already know about gives me no refactoring confidence at all - it is decoration, and I should not present it as safety. Differently: I would order tests by risk rather than by coverage - the revoke interleaving first, then expiry and revocation at the read path, then a forced-conflict test that stubs the code generator so the retry loop actually executes, then negative-cache TTL behaviour. Coverage percentage would go up as a side effect, and I would stop quoting it as if it were the point.

landmines.q: I set X-Forwarded-For to a random value on every request. What does your rate limiter do?
landmines.why: It happily creates a fresh bucket per request. The limiting algorithm is correct and the identity it keys on is attacker-controlled - a candidate who is proud of the atomic Lua script often has not thought about where the key comes from.
landmines.answer: Facts: it gives you an unlimited rate. I derive the per-IP bucket key from X-Forwarded-For with no trusted-proxy allowlist, so a spoofed header gets a fresh bucket every time and the per-IP limit does not exist against anyone who tries. Limitation: I solved the concurrency half correctly - the token bucket is a single atomic Lua script, so there is no lost-update race - and then keyed it on a client-supplied string, which makes the correct half pointless. Differently: parse the header right-to-left, discard hops while they are inside my known proxy CIDR set, take the first untrusted address, and ignore the header entirely if the connection did not arrive from a trusted proxy. And for the write path, key on the API key rather than the network address, since that is the identity I actually control.

landmines.q: Redis is down. What is the blast radius, and did you ever test it?
landmines.why: The cache absorbs 96.8% of reads, so a Redis outage multiplies Postgres load ~30x on a single unreplicated primary; the rate limiter also depends on it and fails open. There is no circuit breaker, no shedding, and this scenario has never been exercised.
landmines.answer: Facts: every read misses, so Postgres goes from about 3% of redirect traffic to 100% - roughly 30x the query volume on one primary with no read replica. The negative cache disappears too, so scanner traffic that costs a Redis lookup today becomes database queries. The rate limiter fails open. I have never tested it. Limitation: I described Redis as a cache but built it as a hard dependency, and I have no evidence about what actually happens - the failure mode I would bet on is connection-pool exhaustion and a cascading timeout, not a graceful slowdown. Differently: a small in-process LRU as a second-level cache so the hottest codes survive a Redis loss, a bounded-concurrency semaphore on database lookups so the outage becomes latency instead of an outage, an explicit fail-closed-or-shed policy for the limiter, and a chaos test that kills Redis under load in staging.

landmines.q: Someone shortens a phishing URL with your service. Then what?
landmines.why: There is no blocklist, no reputation check, no abuse-report path, no interstitial, and no rescanning. A public shortener without abuse controls is not an incomplete product, it is an actively harmful one - and it is the question most side-project shorteners have never considered.
landmines.answer: Facts: nothing stops it and nothing detects it. No destination reputation check at mint time, no blocklist, no abuse-report endpoint, no interstitial, and no rescanning of URLs that were clean when shortened. Limitation: shorteners are attractive to abuse precisely because they hide the destination, so 'nobody uses it yet' is the only thing between this design and being a phishing relay - which is not a security control. Differently, in order: an abuse-report endpoint plus a working revoke, since takedown matters more than prevention and my 302 choice is what makes revocation actually effective; then a reputation lookup at mint time; then periodic rescanning, because a destination can be weaponised after it passes the first check; then an interstitial warning for anything flagged rather than a silent 404, so a user who clicks learns why.

landmines.q: Your dashboard says a link has 41,203 clicks. Is that number right?
landmines.why: The analytics pipeline drops events when the buffer fills, which happens during spikes - so the counts are systematically low, biased toward undercounting the most popular links, and nothing in the UI says so.
landmines.answer: Facts: it is a lower bound, not a count. The redirect handler does a non-blocking send onto an 8,192-slot channel and drops the event if the buffer is full, which happens during traffic spikes - so the undercount is biased exactly toward the links and moments people care about. There is a dropped-event counter and nothing surfaces it. Limitation: presenting an approximate number as exact is worse than presenting no number, because someone will make a decision on it. Differently: expose the drop counter as a metric and alert on it, label the dashboard as approximate whenever drops are non-zero in the window, and change the overflow behaviour from dropping blind to either spilling to a local queue or degrading to a sampled counter with a known sampling rate - a count I can correct for is worth far more than a count that is silently wrong.

ownership_prompts: Which parts of Linkbase did you write from scratch, which did you adapt from a tutorial, blog post, or AI assistant, and which are libraries doing the hard part? Be specific per file - an interviewer can read the repo while you talk.

The move from hash-based to random codes: was that your decision, and what specifically made you change it? Name the moment you realised - a bug, a review comment, an article - not a rationalisation written afterwards.

Describe one bug you personally found and fixed here: the symptom you saw, how you traced it, the actual root cause, and how you verified the fix. Not the invalidation race - that one you only read about in your own code.
You quote 12,000 rps. Who set up that benchmark, what exactly did you configure, and at what point did you notice the setup could not support the claim?
What did you build that you would now delete, and why? A project with nothing you regret is a project you have not thought about since you finished it.
In one honest sentence: why did you build a URL shortener at all, and what did you actually learn that you did not know before?
fixlist: Delete the '99.9% uptime' line from the README, and either footnote the 12,000 rps benchmark with its exact conditions (same-host laptop, loopback, no TLS, 1,000-URL working set) or remove the number. An interviewer who reads the README before the call will open on whichever claim is softest.

Add a KNOWN LIMITATIONS section to the README naming the revoke invalidation race, the unbounded click_events table, the happy-path-only tests, the spoofable X-Forwarded-For identity, and the absent abuse controls. Naming your own flaws first converts every landmine in this dossier into evidence of judgement.

Write the one test that reproduces the revoke race (park the reader between SELECT and SET, revoke, release, assert the cache key is gone) and commit it even if it is failing and marked skip. A failing test that documents a known bug is a stronger artefact than a green suite that avoids it.

Rename or delete any leftover scratch files, commented-out experiments, and generated scaffolding still sitting in the repo, and make sure the commit history does not contain a leaked .env or connection string - interviewers grep, and a credential in history is a different conversation than the one you prepared for.

projects.name: Churn Radar - Telecom Churn Prediction (EXAMPLE)
projects.coach_brief:
ROLE: you are an adversarial-but-fair interviewer grilling the candidate on 'Churn Radar', their customer-churn prediction project. Ask ONE question at a time and follow the weakest thing they just said. The central test in this dossier is whether the candidate can identify and own a genuine methodological error in their own work before you spell it out for them. This is EXAMPLE content shipped with the app; a real candidate should have replaced it with their own project.

WHAT IT IS: Churn Radar predicts which telecom subscribers will churn. Python 3.11, pandas, scikit-learn 1.4, imbalanced-learn, XGBoost 2.0, a Streamlit demo, joblib artifacts; ~1,800 LOC plus three notebooks. Dataset: a public telecom-churn dataset from a data-science competition site - 7,043 rows, 21 columns, 1,869 churners (26.54% positive class). Cleaning: 11 blank strings in TotalCharges coerced to numeric (all of them tenure = 0, i.e. brand-new customers who have not been billed); 16 categorical columns one-hot encoded to 45 features. Model: XGBoost, 400 trees, depth 5, learning rate 0.05, chosen by GridSearchCV over 5-fold StratifiedKFold. Reported headline in the README: 94% accuracy, 0.93 F1, 0.98 ROC-AUC.

THE HEADLINE DEFECT - SMOTE BEFORE THE SPLIT: the pipeline calls SMOTE().fit_resample(X, y) on the FULL dataset and then train_test_split(X_res, y_res, test_size=0.2, stratify=y_res). This is target leakage and it is the single most important thing in this dossier. Mechanism the candidate must be able to state precisely: SMOTE creates synthetic minority points by picking a minority sample, choosing one of its k=5 nearest minority neighbours, and interpolating at a random point on the segment between them. Applied before the split, synthetic points generated FROM test-set rows land in the training set, so the model trains on convex combinations of the very rows it is later evaluated on. Additionally the split can separate a synthetic point from its own parent, putting a near-duplicate of a test row into train. The test set is therefore not held out in any meaningful sense, and the metrics are memorisation. GridSearchCV ran on the same contaminated data, so the hyperparameters were also selected against leaked information. The candidate has since redone it correctly - SMOTE inside an imblearn Pipeline so resampling is fit only on the training portion of each CV fold, never on the validation fold - and the numbers moved from 0.94 accuracy / 0.93 F1 / 0.98 ROC-AUC to 0.79 accuracy / 0.62 F1 on the churn class / 0.844 ROC-AUC / 0.65 PR-AUC. Press until they volunteer the mechanism, the magnitude, and the fix; a candidate who says only 'I applied SMOTE too early' without explaining why interpolation leaks has memorised the lesson rather than understood it.

OTHER REAL DEFECTS, in the order to press:
1. ACCURACY ON AN IMBALANCED DATASET. Predicting 'nobody churns' scores 73.46% accuracy on this data. The corrected 0.79 is +5.5 points over that trivial baseline. Accuracy was the wrong headline metric from the start; PR-AUC (baseline = prevalence = 0.265) and recall at a business-chosen threshold are the right ones. Note ROC-AUC is also optimistic under imbalance because the false-positive rate is diluted by a large negative class.
2. NO BASELINE. There was no majority-class baseline, no logistic-regression baseline, and no comparison against whatever heuristic the retention team already uses. When the candidate finally added logistic regression with class_weight='balanced' it scored 0.836 ROC-AUC against XGBoost's 0.844 - the gradient boosting bought 0.008 AUC for a large cost in explainability and operational complexity. Make them defend the model choice against that number.
3. NO CALIBRATION. Resampling shifts the training base rate, so the model's outputs are not probabilities at the true prevalence - they are systematically over-confident toward the positive class. Nothing produced a reliability diagram or a Brier score. The candidate should know the prior-correction relationship (oversampling positives by factor k inflates the predicted odds by roughly k, so corrected_odds = model_odds / k) and that the practical fix is CalibratedClassifierCV (isotonic or Platt) fitted on a held-out calibration set that was never resampled.
4. THE THRESHOLD IS 0.5 BY DEFAULT. The business action is a retention offer costing about $30, the retained margin is about $420, and offers succeed maybe 30% of the time. Expected value of contacting a true churner = 0.30 * 420 - 30 = +$96; contacting a non-churner = -$30. Break-even probability p* = 30 / (0.30 * 420) = 0.238. The correct operating threshold is ~0.24, not 0.5 - a factor-of-two error in who gets contacted, and it only makes sense with calibrated probabilities, which links back to (3).
5. A LEAKING FEATURE. An 'enrichment' column, retention_offer_sent, is the top feature by gain (0.41). It is set by the retention team only AFTER a customer calls to cancel - it is a consequence of churn, not a predictor of it. Any feature recorded after the prediction time is leakage. A subtler one in the same family: tenure and TotalCharges are measured at the customer's END date for churners, so both encode outcome timing; the dataset has no explicit prediction-time cutoff at all.
6. PROVENANCE. The README says 'trained on customer data', which reads as production data from an employer. It is a public competition dataset - a clean, deduplicated, pre-labelled snapshot with no missingness pattern, no drift, and no join keys. Presenting it as production experience is a resume-integrity problem, and it also hides that the candidate has never done the hard part: defining the label, the observation window, and the prediction cutoff.
7. NO OUT-OF-TIME VALIDATION. A random split on a snapshot cannot detect the way churn models decay; there is no month-forward evaluation and no drift monitoring plan.
8. TEST-SET REUSE. The held-out set was scored repeatedly across many tuning iterations, so even the corrected number is mildly optimistic - the classic multiple-comparisons problem, which needs a genuinely untouched final holdout.
9. SHAP AS CAUSATION. The notebook's narrative reads month-to-month contracts as a cause of churn. SHAP explains the MODEL, not the world; it cannot license an intervention.

CALIBRATION FOR GRADING: the strongest possible answer volunteers the SMOTE leak unprompted, explains the interpolation mechanism, quotes both the before and after numbers without flinching, and treats the correction as the most valuable thing the project produced. A good answer gets there with one nudge. A weak answer says 'SMOTE helps with imbalance' and has to be walked into the problem, or defends the 0.94 as 'the training result'. Reward anyone who computes the 73.46% majority baseline or the 0.238 threshold live. Punish spin - 'it still shows the concept' - and punish any claim that the public dataset was production data. Also make sure they will actually DEFEND something: the drop from 0.94 to 0.79 is a demonstration of competence, not a failure, and a candidate who apologises for the entire project has miscalibrated in the other direction.

pitches.s30: Churn Radar predicts telecom subscriber churn on a public dataset of about seven thousand customers with a 26.5% churn rate. The honest headline is the bug: my first version applied SMOTE before the train/test split, which leaks synthetic copies of test rows into training, and it reported 94% accuracy. I rebuilt it with resampling inside a cross-validation pipeline so it only ever fits on the training fold, and the real numbers are 0.844 ROC-AUC, 0.65 PR-AUC against a 0.265 baseline, and 0.79 accuracy against a 73.5% majority-class baseline. Finding and fixing that is the part of the project I would actually put on a resume.
pitches.m2: Churn Radar is a churn classifier on a public telecom dataset - 7,043 customers, 21 columns, 26.5% churn. Pipeline: clean eleven blank TotalCharges values, which turn out to be customers with zero tenure who had never been billed; one-hot encode sixteen categoricals into 45 features; train XGBoost, 400 trees, depth 5, learning rate 0.05, tuned with five-fold stratified cross-validation. My first version reported 94% accuracy, 0.93 F1 and 0.98 ROC-AUC, and all three were wrong. I had called SMOTE on the entire dataset and then split it. SMOTE builds synthetic minority points by interpolating between a sample and one of its five nearest minority neighbours, so resampling before the split puts synthetic points derived from test rows into training - the model literally trains on convex combinations of the rows it is scored on, and the held-out set is no longer held out. Worse, the grid search selected hyperparameters on that same contaminated data. Rebuilt properly - SMOTE inside an imbalanced-learn pipeline so it fits only on each training fold and never touches the validation fold - accuracy fell from 0.94 to 0.79, churn-class F1 from 0.93 to 0.62, ROC-AUC from 0.98 to 0.844. Two other things I got wrong and fixed: I was headlining accuracy on a dataset where predicting nobody churns scores 73.5%, so the real metrics are PR-AUC, 0.65 against a 0.265 prevalence baseline, and recall at a business threshold; and I had no baseline at all, so I added logistic regression with balanced class weights, which scores 0.836 AUC against XGBoost's 0.844 - eight thousandths for a large cost in explainability, which honestly weakens the case for the boosted model. Still open: the probabilities are uncalibrated, so the 0.5 threshold is arbitrary - the expected-value break-even for a thirty-dollar retention offer is about 0.24 - and this is a static public snapshot, not production data, with no out-of-time validation.
pitches.m10:
- Problem framing: predict which subscribers churn so retention spend goes to the people it can actually move - the model is one input to a decision, not the deliverable
- Data, stated honestly: a PUBLIC competition dataset, 7,043 rows x 21 columns, 1,869 churners (26.54%). Not production data. Clean, pre-labelled, no missingness pattern, no drift, no join keys - i.e. none of the hard parts
- Cleaning: 11 blank TotalCharges strings -> all tenure = 0, brand-new customers never billed; keep them with TotalCharges = 0 rather than drop, because 'new customer' is a real and predictive state
- Features: 16 categoricals one-hot to 45 columns; no target encoding, because target encoding without fold-wise fitting is its own leak
- THE BUG, told first and in full: SMOTE().fit_resample(X, y) on the whole dataset, THEN train_test_split.
