SMOTE interpolates between a minority point and one of its k=5 nearest minority neighbours, so synthetic points built from test rows end up in training - the test set is not held out and the metrics are memorisation
- The numbers before and after: 0.94 -> 0.79 accuracy, 0.93 -> 0.62 churn F1, 0.98 -> 0.844 ROC-AUC. The grid search also selected hyperparameters on the leaked data, so even the model config was chosen wrongly
- The fix: imblearn.pipeline.Pipeline([SMOTE, XGB]) inside StratifiedKFold, so resampling is fit on the training portion of each fold only and the validation fold is never resampled or touched
- Metric discipline: majority-class accuracy is 73.46%, so 0.79 is +5.5 points over trivial; report PR-AUC (0.65 vs a 0.265 prevalence baseline) and recall at the operating threshold; ROC-AUC flatters imbalanced problems because the FPR denominator is large
- Baselines I should have had from day one: majority class, and logistic regression with class_weight='balanced' at 0.836 AUC vs XGBoost 0.844 - which is a real argument for shipping the simpler model
- Calibration and threshold: resampling shifts the base rate, so outputs are not probabilities; oversampling positives by k inflates the odds by about k. Offer cost $30, retained margin $420, offer success ~30% -> break-even p* = 30/(0.3*420) = 0.238, so the operating point is ~0.24, not 0.5 - and that only means anything after isotonic calibration on a non-resampled holdout
- Leakage beyond SMOTE: retention_offer_sent is the top feature by gain and is only set after a customer calls to cancel - a consequence of the label; and tenure/TotalCharges are measured at the churn date, so the dataset has no real prediction-time cutoff
- What I would do next, in order: fit calibration and pick the threshold by expected value; build an out-of-time split and a drift check; drop the post-outcome features and re-measure; and only then consider whether the boosted model earns its complexity over logistic regression

read.body:
Churn Radar is a supervised binary classifier that predicts telecom subscriber churn. Python 3.11, pandas, scikit-learn 1.4, imbalanced-learn, XGBoost 2.0, joblib for artifacts, and a small Streamlit demo that scores a single customer form; ~1,800 LOC plus three notebooks (EDA, modelling, evaluation).

The data is a PUBLIC telecom-churn dataset from a data-science competition site: 7,043 rows, 21 columns, 1,869 churners = 26.54% positive class. Columns are customer demographics (gender, senior citizen, partner, dependents), account facts (tenure in months, contract type, paperless billing, payment method, MonthlyCharges, TotalCharges), and service flags (phone, multiple lines, internet type, online security, backup, device protection, tech support, streaming). It is not production data, and the README used to imply otherwise - that wording is now corrected, because the distinction matters: a competition snapshot arrives clean, pre-labelled, deduplicated, with no missingness pattern and no time dimension, which means it skips every genuinely hard part of a real churn problem.

TotalCharges arrives as a string column with 11 blank values. All 11 are rows with tenure = 0 - customers who signed up and had not yet been billed. Dropping them would be the lazy fix; they are kept with TotalCharges = 0, because 'brand-new customer' is a real and predictive state and silently deleting a customer segment is how a model learns a population it will never see in production.

16 categorical columns are one-hot encoded, giving 45 features. Target/mean encoding was deliberately avoided: computing a category's mean target over the whole dataset and then splitting is the same class of leak as the SMOTE bug below, and doing it correctly requires fold-wise fitting inside the cross-validation loop. MonthlyCharges and TotalCharges are correlated by construction (TotalCharges ~ MonthlyCharges * tenure), which matters for interpreting coefficients but not for tree performance.

read.h: The bug: SMOTE before the split
read.body:
The original pipeline was: X_res, y_res = SMOTE().fit_resample(X, y), then train_test_split(X_res, y_res, test_size=0.2, stratify=y_res), then GridSearchCV on the training part. This is target leakage.

MECHANISM: SMOTE synthesises a minority point by selecting a minority sample, choosing one of its k = 5 nearest minority neighbours, and placing a new point at a random position on the segment between them - x_new = x_i + lambda * (x_neighbour - x_i), lambda ~ U(0,1). Run before the split, this produces synthetic points that are convex combinations of rows which later land in the test set. Those synthetic points are shuffled into training. The model therefore trains on interpolations of the exact rows it is scored on, and the held-out set is not held out. A second path makes it worse: a synthetic point and its own parent can be separated by the split, putting a near-duplicate of a test row directly into training.

IMPACT, measured: 0.94 accuracy / 0.93 churn-F1 / 0.98 ROC-AUC became 0.79 / 0.62 / 0.844 once resampling moved inside the cross-validation. And because GridSearchCV also ran on the contaminated data, the selected hyperparameters were chosen against leaked information too - the fix required re-tuning, not just re-scoring.

THE FIX: imblearn.pipeline.Pipeline([('smote', SMOTE()), ('clf', XGBClassifier())]) evaluated with StratifiedKFold. An imbalanced-learn pipeline applies samplers during fit and skips them during predict, so each fold resamples ONLY its training portion and the validation fold is left at its natural prevalence - which is also the only way the validation metrics mean anything, since a resampled validation set has the wrong base rate.

read.h: Metrics: why accuracy was the wrong headline
read.body:
With 26.54% churn, the majority-class classifier - predict 'no churn' for everyone - scores 73.46% accuracy while catching zero churners. Reporting 0.94, or even the corrected 0.79, as a headline invites the only question that matters: how much better than nothing is that? For 0.79 the answer is 5.5 points, which is real but unremarkable, and accuracy hides where the gain came from.

The metrics that carry information here: PR-AUC (0.65) against a baseline equal to the prevalence, 0.265 - precision-recall curves respond to the minority class and do not get diluted by a large true-negative mass; ROC-AUC (0.844) as a ranking-quality measure, with the caveat that it flatters imbalanced problems because the false-positive rate has a large denominator; and precision/recall at the actual operating threshold, since that is what the retention team experiences. At the expected-value threshold of ~0.24 the corrected model gets recall 0.78 at precision 0.51 - i.e. it finds most churners and about half the people it flags are not going to churn, which is a business conversation about offer cost, not a modelling failure.

read.h: Baselines, and what the model actually bought
read.body:
The original project had no baselines at all: no majority-class reference, no simple linear model, and no comparison to whatever rule the retention team already applies (something like 'month-to-month contract and tenure under six months'). Without a baseline, a number is unfalsifiable - 0.844 AUC is meaningless until you know what 5 lines of logistic regression score.

Added afterwards: LogisticRegression(class_weight='balanced') on the same 45 features scores 0.836 ROC-AUC against XGBoost's 0.844. Eight thousandths of AUC. That is a genuinely uncomfortable result and it should be reported first, not buried: for that gap, the linear model gives directly interpretable coefficients, trains in under a second, is trivial to calibrate, has far fewer ways to fail silently in production, and can be explained to a retention manager without a SHAP plot. The honest reading is that this dataset is close to linearly separable in its useful directions and gradient boosting has little left to extract.

The model outputs a score in [0,1]; nothing verified it behaves like a probability. Two reasons it does not. First, resampling changes the training base rate: after SMOTE the classes are balanced, so the model learns P(churn | x) under a 50% prior instead of the true 26.5%, and its outputs are systematically over-confident toward churn. If positives are oversampled by factor k, the predicted odds are inflated by roughly k, so corrected odds = model_odds / k - equivalently, subtract ln(k) from the log-odds. Second, boosted trees are not calibrated out of the box regardless of resampling.

This matters because the deployed decision is not 'churn or not' but 'is this customer worth a retention offer'. With an offer cost of about $30, retained margin of about $420, and an offer success rate of about 30%: expected value of contacting a true churner = 0.30 * 420 - 30 = +$96; contacting a non-churner = -$30. Break-even probability p* = cost / (success_rate * margin) = 30 / 126 = 0.238. So the operating threshold should be ~0.24, not the default 0.5 - roughly twice as many customers contacted. Using an uncalibrated score with an arbitrary threshold gets the decision wrong in both directions at once. The fix is CalibratedClassifierCV with isotonic regression, fitted on a held-out calibration set that has NOT been resampled, checked with a reliability diagram and a Brier score, and only then the expected-value threshold.

read.h: Leakage beyond SMOTE, and what the data cannot support
read.body:
retention_offer_sent, added in an 'enrichment' pass, was the top feature by gain at 0.41. It is set by the retention team only after a customer has called to cancel, so it is a consequence of churn, not a predictor of it - a model leaning on it would look excellent offline and be useless at prediction time, when the value is always 'no'. The general rule: a feature is leakage if its value is not knowable at the moment the prediction must be made, and the only reliable defence is a prediction-time cutoff applied to every feature's recording timestamp.

A subtler version affects the base dataset. For churners, tenure is measured at the point they left and TotalCharges is the sum up to that point, so both encode outcome timing rather than a snapshot at a fixed decision moment. The dataset has no observation window and no cutoff, which means it cannot support a properly framed churn model at all - the label is 'did churn at some unspecified point' rather than 'churned within 60 days of a stated cutoff'.

Two further limits worth stating before someone asks. There is no out-of-time validation: a random split on a static snapshot cannot show model decay, and churn models decay quickly because pricing, competitors and campaigns move. And the test set was scored repeatedly across tuning iterations, so even the corrected 0.844 is mildly optimistic - the multiple-comparisons problem - and a genuinely untouched final holdout would be needed to quote it as an unbiased estimate.

claims.claim: The model achieves 94% accuracy and 0.98 ROC-AUC.
claims.evidence: The original README and notebook output, produced by SMOTE on the full dataset followed by train_test_split.
claims.limitation: Those numbers are the product of target leakage. Synthetic minority points interpolated from test rows were present in training, so the test set was never held out and the metrics measure memorisation.
claims.counter: You published a number that measured your own bug and only found it later - what else in this repo has never been checked?
claims.wording: Those numbers are wrong and I retract them. I resampled with SMOTE before splitting, which puts synthetic points built from test rows into training, so the evaluation was contaminated. Corrected - resampling inside the cross-validation pipeline - it is 0.79 accuracy, 0.62 churn F1, 0.844 ROC-AUC. The correction is the part of this project worth talking about.

claims.claim: Applying SMOTE inside an imblearn Pipeline within StratifiedKFold is the correct way to resample.
claims.evidence: train.py: Pipeline([('smote', SMOTE(k_neighbors=5)), ('clf', XGBClassifier(...))]) scored with cross_val_score over StratifiedKFold(5); imbalanced-learn applies samplers during fit only and bypasses them at predict time.
claims.limitation: Correct resampling makes the evaluation honest but does not make the model good - and it is still an open question whether SMOTE earns its place at all versus simply weighting the loss.
claims.counter: You fixed the plumbing and kept the assumption. On tabular data with 26% positives, scale_pos_weight or class_weight usually matches or beats SMOTE with none of the risk.
claims.wording: The sampler has to live inside the pipeline so it is fit on the training fold only and the validation fold keeps its natural prevalence - otherwise both the training data and the metric are contaminated. I will concede the follow-on point: at 26% positives, SMOTE may not be needed at all, and I have not run the ablation against scale_pos_weight that would settle it.

claims.claim: Accuracy is a meaningful headline metric for this problem.
claims.evidence: None that survives scrutiny - it was the metric the notebook printed first.
claims.limitation: The majority-class classifier scores 73.46% here. The corrected 0.79 is +5.5 points over predicting that nobody ever churns, and accuracy is blind to which class the errors fall on.
claims.counter: You headlined a metric that a one-line constant function nearly matches. That suggests you never asked what the number was being compared against.
claims.wording: It is not. Predicting no-churn for everyone scores 73.46% on this data, so accuracy mostly measures the class balance. The right headline is PR-AUC - 0.65 against a 0.265 prevalence baseline - plus precision and recall at the operating threshold, because that is what the retention team actually experiences.

claims.claim: XGBoost was the right model for this problem.
claims.evidence: 0.844 ROC-AUC after correction, with 400 trees at depth 5 and learning rate 0.05, selected by 5-fold stratified CV.
claims.limitation: Logistic regression with class_weight='balanced' scores 0.836 on the same features - a gap of 0.008 AUC for a large loss in interpretability and operational simplicity.
claims.counter: You paid the complexity of gradient boosting for eight thousandths of AUC. On this dataset the correct engineering decision is the linear model, and the fact that you reached for XGBoost first says the choice was habit rather than evidence.
claims.wording: On this evidence I would not defend it. Logistic regression with balanced class weights is at 0.836 against XGBoost's 0.844, and for that gap the linear model is interpretable, trivial to calibrate, and far harder to break in production. I reached for the boosted model by default and only built the baseline afterwards, which is the wrong order.

claims.evidence: predict_proba outputs are used directly in the Streamlit demo and thresholded at 0.5.
claims.limitation: Resampling balances the training prior, so the outputs correspond to a 50% base rate rather than the true 26.5% - systematically over-confident toward churn. No reliability diagram or Brier score was ever produced, and boosted trees are not calibrated by default anyway.
claims.counter: If the scores are not probabilities, every downstream decision made with them - including your threshold - is arbitrary. You built a ranker and shipped it as a probability estimator.
claims.wording: They are scores, not calibrated probabilities. Oversampling the positive class inflates the predicted odds by roughly the resampling factor, so the outputs sit above the true rate and I never measured by how much. The fix is isotonic calibration on a held-out set that was not resampled, verified with a reliability diagram and Brier score - and until that exists, any threshold I pick is guesswork.

claims.evidence: The default of predict(); nothing else justifies it.
claims.limitation: The business break-even is p* = offer cost / (success rate * retained margin) = 30 / (0.30 * 420) = 0.238. A 0.5 threshold contacts roughly half the customers it should.
claims.counter: 0.5 is a convention from balanced binary classification, not a decision rule. You optimised a model and then discarded the optimisation at the last step.
claims.wording: 0.5 is just the library default and it is wrong here. With a $30 offer, $420 retained margin and a 30% offer success rate, contacting a true churner is worth +$96 and a false positive costs $30, so break-even is 0.238. The operating point should be about 0.24 - and that only makes sense once the scores are calibrated, which they are not yet.

claims.claim: The feature-importance analysis identifies what drives churn.
claims.evidence: XGBoost gain importances plus SHAP summary plots; the notebook narrative reads month-to-month contracts as a driver of churn.
claims.limitation: SHAP explains the model's behaviour, not the world. The top feature by gain (0.41) was retention_offer_sent - a flag set only after a customer calls to cancel - which is a consequence of churn, not a cause of it.
claims.counter: Your most important feature is an effect of the outcome, and you wrote a causal story on top of it. That is exactly how leakage survives review.
claims.wording: It identifies what the model leans on, which is not the same as what drives churn. My top feature by gain was retention_offer_sent, which is only set after someone calls to cancel - a post-outcome flag, so the importance ranking was partly a leakage detector I misread as an insight. SHAP can tell me the model is contract-sensitive; only an experiment can tell me changing contracts changes churn.

claims.claim: This project demonstrates work on production customer data.
claims.evidence: None. The dataset is a public telecom-churn dataset from a competition site: 7,043 rows, 21 columns, 26.54% churn.
claims.limitation: A competition snapshot is clean, pre-labelled, deduplicated, has no missingness pattern, no time dimension, no join keys and no drift - it omits every part of a real churn problem that is actually difficult.
claims.counter: Describing a public benchmark as customer data is a resume-integrity problem, and it also means you have never defined a label, an observation window, or a prediction cutoff.
claims.wording: It is a public competition dataset, not production data, and the README wording implied otherwise, which I have fixed. What I want to be clear about is what that costs the project: I never had to define the label, the observation window, or the prediction cutoff - and those are the decisions that determine whether a churn model is meaningful at all.

claims.claim: 0.844 ROC-AUC is an unbiased estimate of generalisation.
claims.evidence: Corrected pipeline, 5-fold stratified cross-validation, held-out test split.
claims.limitation: The held-out set was scored many times across tuning iterations, so selection pressure has leaked into it; and the split is random over a static snapshot, so it says nothing about performance forward in time.
claims.counter: You reused the test set as a development signal. Even the corrected number is optimistic, and you have no out-of-time evidence at all.
claims.wording: It is mildly optimistic, for two reasons. I scored the same holdout repeatedly while iterating, which is multiple comparisons - the fix is a final holdout touched exactly once. And it is a random split on a snapshot, so it cannot detect decay; a real evaluation would train on earlier months and test on later ones, because churn models degrade as pricing and competition move.

qa.q: Explain exactly how SMOTE works.
qa.a: For each minority-class sample x_i, find its k nearest minority-class neighbours (k = 5 by default) in feature space. Pick one at random, call it x_n, and create a synthetic point x_new = x_i + lambda * (x_n - x_i) with lambda drawn uniformly from [0,1] - a random point on the line segment between them. Repeat until the target minority ratio is reached. It is interpolation, not generation from a learned distribution: the synthetic points lie inside the convex hull of existing minority neighbourhoods, which is why it makes the boundary region denser rather than teaching the model anything new about the class.

qa.q: Why is applying SMOTE before train_test_split leakage? Be precise.
qa.a: Because synthetic points are functions of specific real rows. If x_i or x_n later lands in the test set, then any synthetic point built from it is a convex combination of a test row - and that synthetic point may end up in training. The model then trains on interpolations of the exact rows it will be evaluated on, so the test set is not independent. There is a second, blunter path: the split can separate a synthetic point from its parent, dropping a near-duplicate of a test row straight into training. The consequence is that the test metric measures partial memorisation, and it inflates most for exactly the minority class you were trying to measure.

qa.q: How much did it inflate your numbers?
qa.a: Accuracy 0.94 to 0.79, F1 on the churn class 0.93 to 0.62, ROC-AUC 0.98 to 0.844. The F1 collapse is the tell: the leak specifically manufactures easy minority examples, so it flatters the minority metric hardest - which is precisely the metric I was quoting to argue the model handled imbalance well. The grid search also ran on the contaminated data, so the hyperparameters had been selected against leaked information and I had to re-tune, not just re-score.

qa.q: What is the correct way to resample, and why does the pipeline placement matter?
qa.a: Put the sampler inside an imblearn Pipeline and evaluate that pipeline with cross-validation: Pipeline([('smote', SMOTE()), ('clf', model)]) scored over StratifiedKFold. An imbalanced-learn pipeline applies samplers during fit and skips them during predict/transform, so each fold resamples only its own training portion and the validation fold is never resampled and never seen. Both halves matter: resampling the validation fold would also be wrong, because it would change the base rate and make precision meaningless - precision depends on prevalence, so a balanced validation set reports a precision the model will never achieve in production.

qa.q: Do you even need SMOTE at 26.5% positives?
qa.a: Probably not, and I have not run the ablation that would prove it either way. 26.5% is a mild imbalance - the pathological cases are fraud or ad-click problems at fractions of a percent. For tree ensembles, scale_pos_weight (or class_weight='balanced' for linear models) reweights the loss directly and achieves the same emphasis without inventing data, without the k-neighbour hyperparameter, and without the risk of interpolating across a class boundary in a region where the classes overlap. SMOTE also interpolates one-hot columns into fractional values that correspond to no real customer, which SMOTE-NC exists to address and which I did not use. My honest position: I reached for SMOTE because it is what the tutorials do.

qa.q: What accuracy does a model that predicts 'no churn' for everyone get?
qa.a: 73.46% - one minus the 26.54% prevalence, with 1,869 churners in 7,043 rows. That is the number every accuracy figure on this dataset has to be compared against, which makes my corrected 0.79 a gain of 5.5 points, and made my original 0.94 claim implausible on its face if I had bothered to make the comparison.

qa.q: Why PR-AUC rather than ROC-AUC on imbalanced data?
qa.a: ROC plots TPR against FPR, and FPR = FP / (FP + TN). With a large negative class the denominator is huge, so a big absolute number of false positives barely moves FPR - the curve looks good while the alert list is mostly wrong. Precision-recall uses precision = TP / (TP + FP), which has no TN term at all, so it responds directly to the cost the retention team actually feels. The baselines differ too: a random classifier sits at 0.5 ROC-AUC regardless of balance, but its PR-AUC equals the prevalence, so here 0.265 is the floor and my 0.65 has to be read against that.

qa.q: What baselines should have existed from day one?
qa.a: Three. The majority-class constant (73.46% accuracy, zero recall) which bounds the accuracy conversation. A regularised logistic regression with balanced class weights, which turned out to score 0.836 AUC against XGBoost's 0.844. And whatever heuristic the business already uses - something like 'month-to-month contract and tenure under six months' - because the model has to beat the incumbent decision process, not beat nothing. Without those, 0.844 is an uninterpretable number.

qa.q: Logistic regression scores 0.836 and XGBoost 0.844. Defend the boosted model.
qa.a: I do not think I can, on this evidence. Eight thousandths of AUC is well inside the variation I would expect across CV folds - I should be quoting a fold standard deviation and I do not have one, which itself means I cannot claim the difference is real. Against that gap, the linear model gives signed interpretable coefficients, trains in under a second, calibrates easily, has fewer silent production failure modes, and can be explained to a retention manager without SHAP. The defensible version of my position is: ship the logistic regression, keep the boosted model as a check on whether meaningful non-linear structure appears as data grows.

qa.q: What does calibration mean, and why does resampling break it?
qa.a: A model is calibrated when, among all cases it scores at 0.3, about 30% actually churn. Resampling breaks it by construction: after SMOTE the training set is balanced, so the model learns P(churn | x) under a 50% prior instead of the true 26.5%, and its outputs sit systematically above the true rate. Quantitatively, if positives are oversampled by factor k, the predicted odds are inflated by roughly k, so corrected_odds = model_odds / k - equivalently subtract ln(k) from the log-odds. Separately, gradient-boosted trees are not naturally calibrated even without resampling, because the loss rewards ranking more than probability accuracy.

qa.q: How would you calibrate it, and how would you check?
qa.a: Fit CalibratedClassifierCV on a held-out calibration set that was NOT resampled - the calibrator has to see the true base rate, which is the whole point. Isotonic regression if there are enough samples (it is non-parametric and can correct any monotone distortion, but it overfits on small sets), Platt scaling / sigmoid if not. Check with a reliability diagram - predicted probability bucketed against observed frequency, ideally on the 45-degree line - plus the Brier score, which is the mean squared error of the probabilities and decomposes into calibration and refinement terms. Crucially, calibration usually leaves ROC-AUC unchanged, because it is a monotone transform: it fixes the numbers without changing the ranking, and that is exactly what the threshold decision needs.

qa.q: Derive the correct decision threshold for the retention campaign.
qa.a: Contacting a customer costs the offer, $30. If they were going to churn and the offer works - success rate about 30% - I retain $420 of margin. So the expected value of contacting someone with churn probability p is p * (0.30 * 420) - 30 = 126p - 30. That is positive when p > 30/126 = 0.238. So the operating threshold is about 0.24, not 0.5: at 0.5 I am declining to contact everyone between 0.24 and 0.5, each of whom has positive expected value. The prerequisite is calibration - the formula uses p as a real probability, so an uncalibrated score plugged into it gives a confidently wrong threshold.

qa.q: At that threshold, what does the retention team actually experience?
qa.a: Recall 0.78 at precision 0.51 - the model surfaces about four in five real churners, and about half of the contact list was never going to leave. That sounds bad stated as precision and is correct stated as economics: at a $30 offer cost and $126 expected gain per true churner, roughly one in four being right would still break even, so 51% precision leaves a comfortable margin. The number that would actually change this decision is the offer success rate, which I assumed at 30% and never measured - and which only a holdout experiment can supply.

qa.q: Your top feature by gain is retention_offer_sent. What is wrong with that?
qa.a: It is set by the retention team only after a customer calls to cancel, so it is a consequence of churn, not a predictor. Offline it looks like a superb feature - it is nearly a copy of the label - and at prediction time it is always 'no', so the model's best signal vanishes exactly when it is deployed. The general rule I should have applied from the start: a feature is leakage if its value is not knowable at the moment the prediction must be made. The operational defence is a prediction-time cutoff enforced on every feature's recording timestamp, not a per-feature judgement call.

qa.q: Are tenure and TotalCharges safe features?
qa.a: Not in this dataset, and this is the subtler leak. For churners, tenure is measured at the moment they left and TotalCharges is the sum up to that point, so both encode when the outcome happened rather than a state at a fixed decision time. There is no observation window and no prediction cutoff anywhere in the data, so the label is really 'churned at some unspecified point' rather than 'churned within 60 days of a stated cutoff'. A properly framed version would fix a cutoff date, compute every feature from data strictly before it, and define the label over a fixed horizon after it - and that reframing is not possible with this snapshot.

qa.q: You have 11 blank TotalCharges values. What did you do and why not just drop them?
qa.a: All 11 have tenure = 0 - new customers who had not yet been billed - so the blank is not missing data, it is a meaningful zero. I coerce to numeric and set 0. Dropping them would delete an entire customer state ('just signed up') from training, so the model would never learn it and would meet it constantly in production. Imputing with the mean would be worse still: it would assert that a brand-new customer has an average billing history, which is false in a way that correlates with the label.

qa.q: Why one-hot encoding rather than target encoding?
qa.a: Target encoding replaces a category with the mean of the label within it, which is a function of the target - computed over the full dataset it leaks the label into the features in exactly the same shape as my SMOTE bug. Doing it safely requires fitting the encoding inside each CV fold on the training portion only, with smoothing for rare categories. With 16 low-cardinality categoricals producing only 45 one-hot columns, there was no dimensionality problem worth taking that risk for. Target encoding earns its place at high cardinality - zip codes, device IDs - not here.

qa.q: Why is a random train/test split insufficient for churn?
qa.a: Because churn is a temporal process and a random split lets the model learn from the future to predict the past - a row from March can train a model that is scored on a row from January, and the environment (pricing, promotions, a competitor's launch) is shared across the split so the model gets credit for knowing it. The right evaluation is out-of-time: train on months 1 to N, test on months N+1 onward, which is the only thing that reveals decay. Churn models decay quickly because the drivers move, and my random split is structurally incapable of showing that.

qa.q: You scored the same holdout many times while tuning. Why is that a problem?
qa.a: Because each look uses the holdout as a selection signal, so I am implicitly optimising against it - multiple comparisons. With enough iterations, the best-scoring configuration is partly the one that got lucky on those particular rows, and the reported metric is biased upward by an amount I cannot quantify after the fact. The discipline is three splits: train, validation (used freely for selection), and a final test touched exactly once when everything is frozen. I have train and a reused validation set that I have been calling a test set.

qa.q: SHAP says month-to-month contracts push predictions toward churn. Can the business act on that?
qa.a: Not directly. SHAP attributes a model's output to its inputs - it explains the model, not the world. The correlation is almost certainly confounded: customers who expect to leave choose month-to-month in the first place, so contract type is partly a proxy for pre-existing intent. Moving a customer onto an annual contract might reduce churn, or might just add a cancellation fee to someone who leaves anyway. The only thing that settles it is an experiment: randomise a contract-upgrade offer across a matched population and measure the difference. A model can tell you who to run that experiment on; it cannot tell you what the experiment will find.

qa.q: How would you monitor this model in production?
qa.a: Four layers. Input drift: population stability index or a KS test per feature against the training distribution, alerting when the score crosses a threshold. Prediction drift: track the mean predicted probability and the flagged-rate over time - a sudden move usually means an upstream pipeline change, not a change in customers. Outcome monitoring: labels arrive with a delay equal to the churn horizon, so hold a scoring log and join it to outcomes as they land, then recompute PR-AUC and calibration on a rolling window. Business metrics: retention-offer acceptance and retained margin, measured against a small randomised holdout that never gets contacted - without that holdout you can never distinguish the model working from the market moving.

qa.q: If you restarted this project tomorrow, what would you do differently and in what order?
qa.a: Framing first: fix a prediction cutoff, define the label over an explicit horizon, and compute every feature strictly from data before the cutoff - which would have made both leaks structurally impossible instead of things I had to catch. Then the trivial baselines - majority class and logistic regression - before touching a boosted model, so every later number has something to be compared against. Then the metric and the threshold from the business economics, so I know what I am optimising before I optimise it. Then, and only then, model selection, with the sampler inside the pipeline and an out-of-time split. The model was the part I spent the most time on and it was the part that mattered least.

whiteboard.title: Draw the SMOTE leak: show the row that ends up on both sides
whiteboard.answer:
SETUP. Minority rows A, B, C, D (churners) sit near each other in feature space.

WRONG ORDER (what I shipped):
  1. SMOTE over ALL rows. It picks A, finds its 5 nearest minority neighbours, selects B, and emits S = A + lambda*(B - A), lambda ~ U(0,1). It does this thousands of times.
  2. train_test_split shuffles the UNION of real and synthetic rows.
  3. Outcome: B lands in TEST. S - which is a convex combination of A and B - lands in TRAIN.
  4. The model has now seen a point on the segment through B, and is then scored on B. If lambda happened to be near 1, S is almost exactly B.
  Second failure path: S and its own parent A get separated, so a near-duplicate of a test row sits in training regardless of the interpolation argument.

CORRECT ORDER:
  1. Split FIRST. Test rows are set aside untouched, at their natural 26.5% prevalence.
  2. Fit SMOTE on the training portion only - inside each CV fold, via an imblearn Pipeline, so the validation fold is also never resampled.
  3. Score on data the sampler has never seen and whose base rate is real.

WHY THE MINORITY METRIC MOVES MOST: the leak manufactures easy positives specifically, so churn-class recall and F1 inflate hardest - which is why F1 fell 0.93 -> 0.62 while accuracy fell only 0.94 -> 0.79. The metric I was using to argue I had handled imbalance was the metric the bug corrupted most.

THE GENERAL RULE: anything fitted from data - a sampler, a scaler, an imputer, a target encoder, a feature selector - must be fit on training data only, inside the cross-validation loop. If it has a .fit(), it belongs in the pipeline.

whiteboard.title: Build the confusion matrix at prevalence 26.5% and derive every metric from it
whiteboard.answer:
Take 1,000 customers at 26.5% prevalence: 265 churners, 735 non-churners. Suppose at the chosen threshold the model catches 78% of churners (recall 0.78) with precision 0.51.

  TP = 0.78 * 265 = 207
  FN = 265 - 207 = 58
  FP: precision = TP/(TP+FP) = 0.51 -> TP+FP = 207/0.51 = 406 -> FP = 199
  TN = 735 - 199 = 536

  Accuracy   = (207 + 536)/1000 = 0.743   <-- barely above the 0.735 majority baseline
  Precision  = 207/406 = 0.51
  Recall/TPR = 207/265 = 0.78
  Specificity= 536/735 = 0.729
  FPR        = 199/735 = 0.271
  F1         = 2PR/(P+R) = 2*0.51*0.78/(1.29) = 0.617

THE LESSON IN THE ARITHMETIC: accuracy of 0.743 is almost identical to the do-nothing baseline of 0.735 while the model is catching 207 of 265 churners. Accuracy has hidden the entire value of the model, because the 199 false positives cancel the gain in the sum. This is why the headline must be precision/recall or PR-AUC.

BASELINES TO ANNOTATE ON THE BOARD: random classifier -> ROC-AUC 0.5, PR-AUC = prevalence = 0.265. Majority-class -> accuracy 0.735, recall 0, precision undefined.

WHY ROC FLATTERS: FPR's denominator is 735, so 199 false positives read as only 0.271. Precision's denominator is 406 - the same errors read as 'half your call list is wrong'. Same matrix, two very different stories; pick the one the person doing the work will experience.

whiteboard.title: Derive the expected-value threshold, then the sensitivity of the answer
whiteboard.answer:
PARAMETERS: offer cost C = $30 (paid on every contact); retained margin M = $420 (realised only if the customer would have churned AND the offer works); offer success rate s = 0.30.

EV of contacting a customer with true churn probability p:
  EV(contact) = p * s * M - C = p * (0.30 * 420) - 30 = 126p - 30
  EV(do nothing) = 0
Contact iff 126p - 30 > 0  ->  p* = C / (s*M) = 30/126 = 0.238

So the operating threshold is ~0.24. At the default 0.5 I am refusing to contact everyone in [0.24, 0.5), each of whom has positive expected value - roughly half the profitable population.

SENSITIVITY - which assumption actually matters:
  s = 0.20 -> p* = 30/84  = 0.357
  s = 0.30 -> p* = 30/126 = 0.238
  s = 0.40 -> p* = 30/168 = 0.179
  C = $50, s = 0.30 -> p* = 50/126 = 0.397
The threshold is far more sensitive to the offer success rate than to anything I could gain by improving the model, and s = 0.30 is an assumption I never measured. The highest-value experiment in this project is not a better model - it is a randomised holdout that measures s.

PRECONDITION: this arithmetic uses p as a genuine probability. Feeding it an uncalibrated score - which mine is, because SMOTE balanced the training prior - produces a confidently wrong threshold. Calibrate first, then optimise the threshold, then report expected value per thousand customers rather than AUC.

whiteboard.title: Write the leak-proof pipeline from scratch, and say what each line prevents
whiteboard.answer:
from sklearn.model_selection import StratifiedKFold, cross_validate, train_test_split
from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
from sklearn.preprocessing import OneHotEncoder, StandardScaler
from imblearn.pipeline import Pipeline          # NOT sklearn.pipeline - only this one applies samplers at fit and skips them at predict
from imblearn.over_sampling import SMOTE

# 1. Split ONCE, before anything is fitted. Stratify to keep 26.5% on both sides.
X_dev, X_final, y_dev, y_final = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2, stratify=y, random_state=0)
#    X_final is touched exactly once, at the very end. Every tuning decision uses X_dev only.

pre = ColumnTransformer([
    ('cat', OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown='ignore'), cat_cols),   # fit on train fold only -> unseen categories at score time cannot crash it
    ('num', StandardScaler(), num_cols)])                        # mean/std from the train fold only -> no test statistics leak in

pipe = Pipeline([
    ('pre', pre),
    ('smote', SMOTE(k_neighbors=5, random_state=0)),             # inside the pipeline -> fit on the train fold, skipped at predict
    ('clf', LogisticRegression(class_weight='balanced', max_iter=2000))])

cv = StratifiedKFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=0)
res = cross_validate(pipe, X_dev, y_dev, cv=cv,
                     scoring=['average_precision', 'roc_auc'],   # PR-AUC first; its baseline is the prevalence
                     return_train_score=True)

WHAT EACH CHOICE PREVENTS:
  imblearn Pipeline .............. synthetic points can never be built from validation rows, and the validation fold keeps its true base rate so precision means something
  ColumnTransformer inside pipe .. the encoder and scaler are fit per fold; fitting them up front leaks distributional information from validation into training
  handle_unknown='ignore' ........ a category present only in validation does not raise, so the fold structure does not silently push you back toward fitting on everything
  stratify + StratifiedKFold ..... every fold carries 26.5% positives, so fold metrics are comparable and small-fold variance does not explode
  X_final held back ............... one unbiased estimate at the end, immune to the multiple-comparisons pressure of repeated tuning
  average_precision headline ...... PR-AUC, against a 0.265 baseline, instead of an accuracy number that a constant function nearly matches

AND THE STEP AFTER: calibration must be fit on data that was NOT resampled - so CalibratedClassifierCV wraps the fitted pipeline using a separate calibration split, never inside the SMOTE'd fold.

whiteboard.title: Frame the problem properly: cutoff, observation window, label horizon
whiteboard.answer:
The dataset has no time dimension, which is why both leaks were possible. Draw the timeline a real churn model requires:

  |<--- observation window --->| CUTOFF T |<--- label horizon --->|
     features computed here                  label defined here
     (strictly < T)                          (churn in (T, T+60d]?)

RULES THAT FALL OUT OF THE PICTURE:
  1. Every feature must be computable from data with a timestamp strictly before T. If a column's recording time is unknown, it is not usable - retention_offer_sent fails this immediately, since it is written after the cancellation call.
  2. The label is 'churned within a fixed horizon after T', not 'ever churned'. A fixed horizon is what makes the positive rate stable across cutoffs and makes the model's output actionable - the retention team acts within a window, so the model must predict within one.
  3. tenure must be tenure AS OF T, not tenure at the end of the relationship. In this snapshot it is the latter for churners, which encodes outcome timing directly into a feature.
  4. Customers who churn before T are excluded; customers who join after T are excluded. Both are population-definition decisions that change the prevalence and therefore every precision number.
  5. Validation is by cutoff, not by random row: train on T1, evaluate at T2 > T1. That is the only structure that can show decay.

WHY THIS IS THE REAL LESSON: with this framing, SMOTE-before-split is still wrong but the fatal feature leaks become structurally impossible - a feature either has a timestamp before T or it does not enter the matrix. The public snapshot let me skip the framing entirely, and every methodological error in this project traces back to that omission rather than to the modelling.

whiteboard.title: Sketch the reliability diagram before and after calibration, and read it
whiteboard.answer:
AXES: x = predicted probability, bucketed into deciles; y = observed churn frequency within each bucket. The diagonal y = x is perfect calibration. Plot the bucket counts underneath as a histogram - a beautiful curve over an empty bucket means nothing.

BEFORE (SMOTE-trained, uncalibrated): the curve sits BELOW the diagonal across the middle of the range - the model predicts 0.60 for groups that churn at roughly 0.35. That is the expected direction: training on a 50/50 resampled set teaches a 50% prior where reality is 26.5%, so every score is pushed up. The distribution of scores is also pushed toward the extremes, because boosting drives training margins up.

THE ANALYTIC CORRECTION: oversampling positives by factor k multiplies the predicted odds by about k. So corrected_odds = model_odds / k, i.e. logit_corrected = logit_model - ln(k). With SMOTE taking 26.5% up to 50%, k ~ (0.5/0.5)/(0.265/0.735) ~ 2.77, so ln(k) ~ 1.02 - subtract roughly one unit of log-odds. This is a first-order fix and assumes the distortion is a pure prior shift, which it is not entirely.

AFTER (isotonic calibration on a non-resampled holdout): the curve should track the diagonal within the noise of each bucket's count. Report the Brier score - mean squared error between predicted probability and the 0/1 outcome - alongside it, since the diagram is qualitative and the Brier score is the single number.

THE KEY PROPERTY: isotonic and Platt scaling are monotone transforms, so ROC-AUC is unchanged. Calibration does not improve the ranking - it makes the numbers mean what they say. Which is exactly what the 0.238 expected-value threshold needs, and why calibration is a prerequisite for the decision, not a cosmetic step.

landmines.q: Show me where SMOTE is called in your code. Now show me where you split.
landmines.why: The original code resamples the full dataset and then splits, which is textbook target leakage, and every headline number in the README came from it. This is the single question that decides the interview - whether the candidate owns it in full, with the mechanism, or has to be dragged there line by line.
landmines.answer: Facts: in the original version SMOTE ran on the entire dataset and train_test_split came after it. That is leakage: SMOTE interpolates between a minority sample and one of its five nearest minority neighbours, so synthetic points built from rows that later land in test end up in training - the model trains on convex combinations of the rows it is scored on, and the split can also separate a synthetic point from its parent, dropping a near-duplicate of a test row into training. The numbers it produced - 0.94 accuracy, 0.93 churn F1, 0.98 AUC - were memorisation. Limitation: I did not catch it myself for weeks, and the grid search ran on the same contaminated data, so the hyperparameters were also selected against leaked information. Corrected - SMOTE inside an imblearn pipeline, fit on the training fold only, validation fold never resampled - it is 0.79 accuracy, 0.62 F1, 0.844 AUC. Differently: the rule I now apply without exception is that anything with a .fit() goes inside the pipeline and the pipeline is what gets cross-validated. And the tell I ignored at the time was the result itself - a 0.98 AUC on a noisy human-behaviour problem should have been treated as a bug report, not a success.

landmines.q: You report 79% accuracy. What does a model that always predicts 'no churn' get?
landmines.why: 73.46%. The headline metric is barely above a constant function, and quoting accuracy on a 26.5%-positive dataset shows the candidate never asked what their number was being compared against.
landmines.answer: Facts: 73.46%, since the prevalence is 26.54%. So my 0.79 is 5.5 points over a model that catches literally zero churners, and accuracy is blind to which class the errors land on. Limitation: accuracy was the wrong headline from the start - it mostly measures the class balance here, and I led with it in the README. Differently: I report PR-AUC as the headline, 0.65 against a baseline equal to the prevalence of 0.265, plus precision and recall at the actual operating threshold, because that is what the retention team experiences - and I state the majority-class baseline next to every accuracy figure so the comparison cannot be avoided.

landmines.q: Your model outputs 0.62 for a customer. Is that customer 62% likely to churn?
landmines.why: No - resampling balanced the training prior, so the outputs correspond to a 50% base rate rather than the true 26.5% and are systematically over-confident. Nothing was ever calibrated or checked, yet the Streamlit demo presents the score as a probability and thresholds it at 0.5.
landmines.answer: Facts: no. Training on a resampled set teaches a 50% prior where the reality is 26.5%, so the outputs sit above the true rate - if positives are oversampled by factor k the odds are inflated by roughly k, and here k is about 2.8, so roughly one unit of log-odds. On top of that, boosted trees are not calibrated by default. Limitation: I never produced a reliability diagram or a Brier score, so I cannot even tell you the size of the distortion, and my demo presents that score to a user as a probability. Differently: fit isotonic calibration on a held-out set that was NOT resampled, verify with a reliability diagram plus Brier score, and only then use the number in a decision. It matters because the actual decision is an expected-value comparison - break-even is about 0.24 for a $30 offer - and that formula needs a real probability, not a ranking score.

landmines.q: Your most important feature is retention_offer_sent. When does that field get set?
landmines.why: After the customer calls to cancel. It is a consequence of the label, it sits at the top of the gain ranking at 0.41, and the notebook built a causal narrative on top of the importance plot without ever asking when the field is written.
landmines.answer: Facts: it is set by the retention team only after a customer has already called to cancel, so it is a consequence of churn rather than a predictor. That is why it dominated the gain ranking at 0.41 - it is close to a copy of the label - and at prediction time it is always 'no', so the model's strongest signal disappears in deployment. Limitation: I read a leakage signal as an insight and wrote it up as a finding, which is worse than missing it. Differently: the rule is that a feature is unusable if its value is not knowable at the moment the prediction must be made, and the way to enforce it is structural, not per-feature judgement - fix a prediction cutoff and compute every feature strictly from data timestamped before it. Under that framing the column could never have entered the matrix.

landmines.q: Where did this data come from?
landmines.why: A public competition dataset, while the README said 'customer data' - which reads as production data from an employer. It is both an integrity issue and a concealment of the fact that the candidate never had to define a label, an observation window, or a prediction cutoff.
landmines.answer: Facts: it is a public telecom-churn dataset from a competition site - 7,043 rows, 21 columns, 26.54% churn. Not production data, and I have never had access to production customer data. Limitation: my README said 'trained on customer data', which implies something it should not, and I have corrected the wording. The deeper cost is what the dataset let me skip: it arrives clean, pre-labelled, deduplicated, with no missingness pattern, no join keys and no time dimension, so I never had to define the label, the observation window, or the prediction cutoff - and those decisions determine whether a churn model means anything at all. Differently: I state the source in the first line of the README, and I would rebuild the project against a dataset with timestamps so the framing has to be done properly.

landmines.q: What does logistic regression score on this data?
landmines.why: 0.836 AUC against XGBoost's 0.844. The baseline was added after the fact, and it substantially undermines the case for the boosted model - many candidates try to talk their way past the 0.008 rather than accept what it implies.
landmines.answer: Facts: 0.836 with class_weight='balanced', against 0.844 for XGBoost. Eight thousandths, and I do not have the fold-level standard deviation, which means I cannot claim the difference is real at all. Limitation: I built the boosted model first and the baseline afterwards, which is the wrong order - without a baseline, 0.844 was an uninterpretable number for as long as it stood alone. Differently: I would ship the logistic regression. For that gap it gives interpretable signed coefficients, trains in a second, calibrates cleanly, and has far fewer silent production failure modes; the boosted model stays as a periodic check on whether real non-linear structure has appeared as the data grows. This dataset appears to be close to linearly separable in its useful directions, and the honest conclusion is that gradient boosting had little left to extract.

landmines.q: This model goes live in January. How does it perform in June?
landmines.why: Unknown - the split is random over a static snapshot, so there is no out-of-time evaluation and no drift monitoring. Churn models decay fast, and a random split is structurally incapable of showing it.
landmines.answer: Facts: I do not know, and my evaluation cannot tell me. The split is random over a snapshot with no time dimension, so a row from later can train a model scored on a row from earlier and the shared environment - pricing, promotions, a competitor's launch - is present on both sides. Limitation: churn drivers move quickly, so a random-split number is close to the best case and the decay curve is invisible to me. Differently: an out-of-time split - train on months 1..N, evaluate at N+1 and N+3 - to measure decay directly, plus monitoring in production: PSI on the input distributions, the flagged-rate over time (a jump there usually means an upstream pipeline change rather than a change in customers), and rolling PR-AUC and calibration recomputed as labels arrive after the horizon. And a small randomised no-contact holdout, because without it I can never separate the model working from the market moving.

landmines.q: How many times did you look at your test set?
landmines.why: Many, across every tuning iteration - so even the corrected 0.844 carries selection pressure. Candidates who have just conceded one leak often defend the corrected number as if it were now clean.
landmines.answer: Facts: many times - I used it as the signal while iterating on features and hyperparameters. That is multiple comparisons: the best-scoring configuration is partly the one that got lucky on those specific rows, so 0.844 is optimistic by an amount I cannot quantify after the fact. Limitation: what I have is a train set and a reused validation set that I have been calling a test set - I do not currently possess an unbiased estimate of generalisation, even after fixing the SMOTE bug. Differently: three splits - train, validation used freely for selection, and a final holdout scored exactly once when everything is frozen - and if I break that rule, the split is burned and I say so rather than quoting the number as if the rule had held.

landmines.q: Your write-up says month-to-month contracts cause churn. Does moving customers to annual contracts reduce churn?
landmines.why: SHAP explains the model, not the world, and contract type is heavily confounded with pre-existing intent to leave. A candidate who cannot separate a model attribution from a causal claim will make expensive recommendations with total confidence.
landmines.answer: Facts: I cannot tell you that from this model. SHAP attributes the model's output to its inputs - it describes what the model leans on, not what would happen if you intervened. Contract type is confounded with intent: customers who already expect to leave choose month-to-month, so the feature is partly a proxy for the outcome's cause rather than a cause itself. Limitation: my notebook narrative reads the importance plot as a causal finding, and that is the kind of claim that gets acted on and wastes budget. Differently: I would phrase it as 'month-to-month is the strongest correlate in the model' and then propose the experiment that could actually answer it - randomise a contract-upgrade offer across a matched population and measure the difference in churn between arms. The model's legitimate role is choosing who to run that experiment on.

ownership_prompts: Which parts of this pipeline did you write yourself, which came from a tutorial or notebook you adapted, and which came from an AI assistant? Be specific per file - the code style will tell an interviewer more than your answer will.

How did you actually discover the SMOTE leak? Name the moment - a review comment, an article, a suspicious result you chased - and say honestly how long the wrong numbers were in the README before you found it.
You quote a $30 offer cost, $420 retained margin, and a 30% offer success rate. Where did each of those three numbers come from, and which of them did you invent?

Which of the corrections in this dossier did you make on your own initiative, and which were pointed out to you? An interviewer will trust the second answer more than a claim that you found everything yourself.
What did you personally learn from this project that changed how you work - not a fact you can recite, but a habit you now have that you did not have before?
In one honest sentence: what is this project actually evidence of? Say it without overclaiming and without dismissing it.
fixlist: Rewrite the README headline: state the dataset is a public competition dataset in the first line, replace the 94% accuracy claim with the corrected PR-AUC alongside the 0.265 prevalence baseline and the 73.46% majority-class accuracy, and put the SMOTE correction in the summary rather than a footnote. The correction is the strongest thing in the repo - burying it makes it look like something you hoped nobody would find.

Keep the buggy pipeline in the repo as a clearly-labelled file (leaky_pipeline_DO_NOT_USE.py) with a comment explaining the interpolation mechanism, and commit the before/after metrics side by side. A visible, documented correction is far better evidence of judgement than a clean history that hides it.

Delete the retention_offer_sent column from the feature set and re-run everything, or if you keep it, gate it behind an explicit prediction-time cutoff check. Leaving a post-outcome feature at the top of your importance plot means the plot in your README is currently arguing against you.

Add the two missing baselines to the results table - majority class and logistic regression with balanced class weights - with fold-level standard deviations, so the 0.008 AUC gap is visible rather than something an interviewer discovers by asking. Reporting a result that weakens your model choice is the single most credible thing a table can do.
